readings.title: Rates And Returns Module 1.1: Interest Rates And
readings.summary:
Interest rates serve three equivalent interpretations: they are required rates of return (the minimum return investors demand to lend funds), discount rates (used to convert future cash flows to present value), and opportunity costs (the return forgone by choosing current consumption over saving). These equilibrium rates reflect the time value of money and differ across securities based on their risk profiles.

The foundation of any interest rate is the real risk-free rate, which represents pure time preference — how much more people value consumption today versus the future, with no inflation or default risk assumed. In practice, observed rates like T-bill yields are nominal risk-free rates because they embed an inflation premium. The precise relationship is: (1 + nominal) = (1 + real)(1 + inflation), commonly approximated as nominal ≈ real + inflation.

Beyond the nominal risk-free rate, three additional risk premiums adjust the required return upward: a default risk premium (compensates for borrower non-payment), a liquidity risk premium (compensates for difficulty selling quickly at fair value), and a maturity risk premium (compensates for greater price volatility in longer-term bonds). The full required return equals the nominal risk-free rate plus all applicable premiums.

Return measurement uses several approaches. The holding period return (HPR) measures total return over a single period: (ending value + income) / beginning value − 1. For multi-period compounding, HPRs are linked multiplicatively. The arithmetic mean is the simple average of periodic returns and is best for estimating a single-period expected return. The geometric mean is the compound annual growth rate across multiple periods and is always ≤ the arithmetic mean. The harmonic mean is used for dollar-cost averaging scenarios (same dollar amount invested each period) and is always ≤ the geometric mean. The inequality holds: harmonic ≤ geometric ≤ arithmetic, with equality only when all values are identical.
key_points: Interest rates have three equivalent interpretations: required rate of return, discount rate, and opportunity cost of consumption — know all three for item-set vignettes.
Nominal risk-free rate = real risk-free rate + inflation premium (exact: multiply the factors; approximate: add them). T-bill rates are nominal, NOT real.
Required return = nominal risk-free rate + default risk premium + liquidity risk premium + maturity risk premium. Each premium compensates for a distinct risk.
HPR = (Ending Value + Income) / Beginning Value − 1. For multi-period HPR, multiply the individual period wealth relatives: (1+R1)(1+R2)...(1+Rn) − 1.
Use arithmetic mean for single-period expected returns; use geometric mean to express compound growth across multiple periods (geometric mean ≤ arithmetic mean).
Use the harmonic mean for average cost per share when investing a fixed dollar amount periodically (e.g., $1,000/month): N / Σ(1/Xi).
Inequality to memorize: harmonic mean ≤ geometric mean ≤ arithmetic mean, with equality only when all observations are identical.

readings.title: The Time Value Of Money In Finance
readings.summary:
Reading 2 applies time value of money (TVM) mechanics to real financial instruments across fixed income, equity, foreign exchange, and derivatives. The core relationship is FV = PV × (1 + r)^t for discrete compounding, or FV = PV × e^(rt) for continuous compounding. Mastery of the TI BA II Plus calculator — with P/Y set to 1 — is essential for exam success, as manual algebraic solutions are too slow under exam conditions.

For fixed-income securities, the reading covers zero-coupon bonds (priced as PV of a single future cash flow), fixed-coupon bonds (PV of an annuity of coupon payments plus PV of par at maturity), perpetuities (PV = PMT/r), and amortizing bonds (level payments covering both interest and principal). A bond trades at a premium when its coupon rate exceeds its yield to maturity, and at a discount when the opposite is true. Negative yields produce zero-coupon bond prices above face value.

Equity valuation follows the same PV logic. Preferred stock paying a fixed dividend is valued as a perpetuity (P = D/r). Common stock is valued using the Gordon Growth Model (P = D1 / (r − g)), where D1 is the next dividend, r is the required return, and g is the constant dividend growth rate. These models require r > g to produce a meaningful (positive, finite) price.

TVM also appears in foreign exchange (covered interest rate parity links spot and forward FX rates through interest rate differentials) and options pricing (the no-arbitrage forward price of an asset equals its spot price compounded at the risk-free rate). These cross-topic applications reinforce that discounting and compounding are universal valuation tools throughout the CFA curriculum.
key_points: Set TI BA II Plus P/Y = 1 before all TVM problems; enter cash outflows as negative (PV for purchases) and inflows as positive (FV for receipts).
Zero-coupon bond price: PV = FV / (1 + r)^t. A negative yield means the bond prices above face value (premium).
Coupon bond value: N, PMT (= coupon rate × par), FV (= par), I/Y (= YTM) → CPT PV. Bond price and YTM move inversely; price > par when coupon rate > YTM.
Perpetuity PV = PMT / r. Preferred stock valued as perpetuity: P = D / r.
Gordon Growth Model for common stock: P = D1 / (r − g), where D1 = next period dividend, r = required return, g = constant growth rate. Only valid when r > g.
Amortizing bond / loan payment: solve for PMT with FV = 0. Each payment covers interest first; the remainder reduces principal.
Covered interest rate parity (FX): Forward rate = Spot × (1 + r_domestic) / (1 + r_foreign). No-arbitrage forward asset price = Spot × (1 + r_f)^t.

readings.title: Statistical Measures Of Asset Returns
readings.summary: This reading covers the core statistical tools used to describe and analyze investment return data. Measures of central tendency — arithmetic mean, median, and mode — each serve different purposes. The arithmetic mean is the most common but is sensitive to outliers. The median, the middle value in an ordered dataset, is preferred when outliers are present. The mode identifies the most frequent value and is most useful for discrete or grouped data. For dealing with outliers, two techniques are introduced: the trimmed mean (drops the most extreme observations by a stated percentage) and the winsorized mean (replaces extreme values with boundary percentile values rather than discarding them).
key_points: Arithmetic mean uses all data points (sum / n) and is sensitive to outliers; median is the middle value and is resistant to outliers — know when each is more appropriate.
Trimmed mean removes a stated % of extreme observations from both tails; winsorized mean replaces extreme values with the boundary percentile value — both reduce outlier impact but in different ways.
Quantiles divide a distribution into equal parts: quartiles (4), quintiles (5), deciles (10), percentiles (100). The interquartile range (IQR) = Q3 − Q1 and measures middle-50% spread.
Sample variance uses n−1 in the denominator (not n) to produce an unbiased estimator of population variance; sample standard deviation is the square root of sample variance.
Dispersion measures risk around the central tendency: range = max − min; MAD = average of absolute deviations from the mean; variance and standard deviation are the most important for exam purposes.
Coefficient of variation (CV) = standard deviation / mean — it measures risk per unit of return, allowing comparison across assets with different means. Lower CV is better.
A box and whisker plot visualizes the IQR (the box), the median, and the full data range (whiskers); asymmetry in whisker length signals potential outliers or skewness.

readings.title: Probability Trees And Conditional Expectations
readings.summary: This reading introduces the concept of expected value as the probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes for a random variable. To calculate it, multiply each possible outcome by its probability and sum the results. This applies directly to investment problems such as estimating expected earnings per share (EPS) or expected portfolio returns under different economic scenarios.
key_points: Expected value formula: E(X) = Σ P(xi) × xi — multiply each outcome by its probability and sum across all outcomes.

Variance (probability model) = Σ P(xi) × [xi − E(X)]²; standard deviation = √variance. Unlike sample statistics, no division by n−1 since no observations exist — probability weights replace sample frequencies.
A probability tree maps joint probabilities by multiplying along branches (e.g., P(good economy) × P(good results | good economy)); all terminal branch probabilities must sum to 1.
Conditional expected value revises an expected outcome based on new information (e.g., expected stock return given a tariff is imposed differs from the unconditional expected return).
Bayes' formula: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B), where P(B) = P(B|A)×P(A) + P(B|Aᶜ)×P(Aᶜ) by the total probability rule.

In Bayes' problems, always build a probability tree first: compute all joint probabilities, identify the conditioning event's total probability by summing relevant branches, then divide the target joint probability by that total.
Key distinction: prior probability is what you believed before new information; posterior (updated) probability is what you believe after applying Bayes' formula to the new information.

readings.title: Portfolio Mathematics Module 5.1: Probability Models
readings.summary:
This reading covers the foundational mathematics for measuring portfolio return and risk. The expected return of a portfolio is the weighted average of the expected returns of its component assets, where each weight equals the market value of that asset divided by the total portfolio value. Understanding how to calculate and interpret these weights is the starting point for all portfolio-level analysis.

Covariance measures how two assets' returns move together. A positive covariance means the assets tend to move in the same direction relative to their means; a negative covariance means they tend to move in opposite directions. The covariance of an asset with itself equals its variance. A covariance matrix organizes all pairwise covariances for a set of n assets, with variances on the diagonal and n(n−1)/2 unique off-diagonal covariance terms. Correlation standardizes covariance by dividing by the product of the two assets' standard deviations, producing a value bounded between −1 and +1.

Portfolio variance is calculated using asset weights, individual variances, and pairwise covariances (or equivalently, correlations). For a two-asset portfolio, variance equals the sum of each asset's squared weight times its variance, plus twice the product of both weights and their covariance. The key insight is that lower (or negative) covariances reduce portfolio variance, forming the quantitative basis for diversification. This extends to three or more assets by adding additional cross-covariance terms.

Covariance and correlation can also be computed from a joint probability function by weighting the product of each asset's deviation from its mean by the probability of each scenario. This approach is used when scenario-based return distributions are given rather than historical data.

Roy's safety-first criterion addresses shortfall risk — the probability that portfolio returns fall below a minimum acceptable threshold return (R_L). The optimal portfolio is the one that maximizes the safety-first ratio: (E(R_p) − R_L) / σ_p. Assuming normally distributed returns, this ratio equals the number of standard deviations the expected return sits above the threshold, and the portfolio with the highest ratio has the lowest probability of falling below R_L.
key_points: Portfolio expected return = weighted average of individual asset expected returns: E(R_p) = Σ w_i × E(R_i)
Portfolio variance for two assets: σ²_p = w²_A σ²_A + w²_B σ²_B + 2w_A w_B Cov(R_A, R_B); lower/negative covariance reduces portfolio risk
Covariance matrix: diagonal terms are variances; off-diagonal terms are covariances; for n assets there are n variances and n(n−1)/2 unique covariances
Correlation = Cov(R_A, R_B) / (σ_A × σ_B); bounded [−1, +1]; can substitute into variance formula using a correlation matrix instead of covariance matrix
To compute covariance from a joint probability function: Cov(R_A, R_B) = Σ P(s) × [R_A(s) − E(R_A)] × [R_B(s) − E(R_B)] across all scenarios
Shortfall risk = probability that portfolio return falls below threshold R_L; minimized by maximizing Roy's safety-first ratio = [E(R_p) − R_L] / σ_p

The safety-first ratio is interpreted as the number of standard deviations between the expected return and the threshold; higher ratio = lower shortfall probability on the standard normal distribution

readings.title: Simulation Methods Module 6.1: Lognormal
readings.summary:
The lognormal distribution arises when a variable x is normally distributed and we take e^x. Because asset prices cannot go below zero, the lognormal distribution is well-suited for modeling them. The key link is that if an asset's future price equals its current price multiplied by e raised to a continuously compounded return, and that return is normally distributed (or approximately so via the central limit theorem), then the future price is lognormally distributed. This is why lognormal is the standard assumption in option pricing and other asset valuation models.

Two important assumptions often made about returns in pricing models are that they are independently distributed (past returns don't predict future returns) and identically distributed (mean and variance are constant over time — a property called stationarity). Together these are the i.i.d. assumptions. They underpin many quantitative finance models and are worth recognizing on the exam.

Monte Carlo simulation generates a distribution of security values by repeatedly drawing random values for risk factors (e.g., stock prices, interest rates) from assumed probability distributions and plugging them into a pricing model. After thousands of iterations, the mean of the resulting distribution serves as the estimated security value. Monte Carlo is highly flexible — it can value complex derivatives, estimate VaR, and test scenarios outside historical experience — but it is a statistical rather than analytic method, and its output quality depends entirely on the quality of the input assumptions.

Bootstrap resampling is an alternative simulation approach that draws its inputs from observed historical data rather than assumed distributions. Repeated sub-samples of size n are drawn with replacement from the original sample, allowing observations to appear more than once. The standard deviation of the resulting sample means estimates the standard error of the sample mean. While bootstrapping avoids the need to specify a theoretical distribution, its outputs are inherently constrained by the historical data used — it cannot model scenarios that never occurred.
key_points: If x is normal, then e^x is lognormal; asset prices are modeled as lognormal because PT = P0 * e^(r), where r is a normally distributed continuously compounded return.
Lognormal distribution is bounded below by zero (no negative asset prices) and is right-skewed — mean > median > mode.
i.i.d. returns: 'independently distributed' means past returns don't predict future returns; 'identically distributed' means constant mean and variance (stationarity).
Monte Carlo simulation: specify distributions for risk factors → randomly generate values → price the security → repeat thousands of times → use mean of distribution as estimate.
Monte Carlo limitation: it is statistical (not analytic), cannot provide closed-form insights, and output is only as good as the assumed distributions and pricing model.
Monte Carlo advantage: inputs are NOT limited to historical data, so analysts can stress-test scenarios that have never occurred.

Bootstrap resampling draws repeated samples WITH replacement from observed data; the standard deviation of sample means estimates standard error — but unlike Monte Carlo, inputs are constrained by actual historical outcomes.

readings.title: Estimation And Inference Module 7.1: Sampling Techniques
readings.summary:
Sampling methods fall into two broad categories: probability sampling (where each population member has a known selection probability) and nonprobability sampling (where selection is based on convenience or researcher judgment). Probability methods include simple random sampling, stratified random sampling, cluster sampling, and systematic sampling. Nonprobability methods include convenience sampling and judgmental sampling. In general, less randomness in the selection process leads to greater sampling error.

Simple random sampling gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection. Stratified random sampling divides the population into subgroups (strata) based on distinguishing characteristics, then randomly samples from each stratum proportionally. This is widely used in bond indexing to ensure representation across key risk factor categories (e.g., maturity, coupon rate) without having to replicate an entire index. The key advantage is that it guarantees proportional representation from every stratum, unlike simple random sampling.

Cluster sampling also uses subgroups, but assumes each cluster is representative of the whole population. In one-stage cluster sampling, entire clusters are selected; in two-stage, random samples are drawn from within selected clusters. Cluster sampling trades accuracy for cost efficiency — it generally produces more sampling error than simple random or stratified sampling, but is cheaper and faster. It is best suited for pilot studies or situations where cost constraints are significant.

Nonprobability methods introduce researcher-driven biases. Convenience sampling uses whatever data is easily accessible, while judgmental sampling relies on the researcher's expertise to hand-pick observations. Both can produce higher sampling error than probability methods, though judgmental sampling by a skilled analyst may sometimes yield a more focused, representative sample for a specific question.

The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is foundational to statistical inference. It states that for samples of size n ≥ 30 drawn from any population with mean μ and finite variance σ², the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution with mean μ and variance σ²/n. This allows analysts to make probability statements and construct confidence intervals about population means regardless of the underlying population distribution, as long as the sample is sufficiently large.
key_points: Probability sampling (simple random, stratified, cluster) gives each member a known selection probability; nonprobability sampling (convenience, judgmental) does not — less randomness = more sampling error.
Stratified random sampling guarantees proportional representation from every stratum; it is the preferred method for bond index replication (stratify by maturity, coupon, duration, etc.).

Cluster sampling assumes each cluster mirrors the whole population; one-stage (all items in selected clusters) has less sampling error than two-stage (random items from selected clusters); lower cost is its main advantage.
Central Limit Theorem: for n ≥ 30, the distribution of sample means is approximately normal with mean μ and variance σ²/n, regardless of the population's distribution.
Standard error of the sample mean = σ/√n (or s/√n when population σ is unknown); larger samples produce smaller standard errors and more precise estimates.

When sampling across a structural break (e.g., pre- vs. post-financial-crisis bank data), pooling data from different regimes produces a sample mean that is not representative of either period — always check for distributional stability.
Systematic sampling (every nth member) is an approximation of simple random sampling and is acceptable when the population has no periodic pattern that aligns with the sampling interval.

readings.title: Hypothesis Testing Module 8.1: Hypothesis Testing
readings.summary:
Hypothesis testing is a statistical procedure used to evaluate whether a statement about a population parameter is reasonable based on sample data. The process begins by stating a null hypothesis (H0), which always includes an 'equal to' condition and represents what the researcher wants to reject, and an alternative hypothesis (Ha), which represents the conclusion drawn if H0 is rejected. These two hypotheses are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, covering all possible outcomes.

The test statistic is the core calculation: it measures the difference between the sample statistic and the hypothesized population value, scaled by the standard error. The critical value—derived from the appropriate distribution (z, t, chi-square, or F)—serves as the benchmark. If the computed test statistic falls in the rejection region (beyond the critical value), the null is rejected; otherwise, we fail to reject it. For a two-tailed z-test at α = 0.05, the critical values are ±1.96.

Two types of errors can occur in hypothesis testing. A Type I error is rejecting a true null hypothesis; its probability equals the significance level α. A Type II error is failing to reject a false null hypothesis. The power of a test is defined as 1 − P(Type II error), representing the probability of correctly rejecting a false null. There is an inherent trade-off: lowering α (reducing Type I error risk) increases the probability of a Type II error and reduces the power of the test.

Sample size plays a critical role in managing error probabilities. Increasing the sample size reduces the standard error of the test statistic, which simultaneously lowers both Type I and Type II error probabilities—effectively increasing the power of the test without sacrificing significance level. Researchers should choose the test statistic that yields the most powerful test among valid alternatives.
key_points: The null hypothesis (H0) ALWAYS includes the 'equal to' condition (e.g., H0: μ = μ0); the alternative hypothesis (Ha) is what you are trying to support.
Type I error = rejecting a TRUE null (probability = α, the significance level); Type II error = failing to reject a FALSE null (probability = β).
Power of a test = 1 − P(Type II error) = probability of correctly rejecting a false null; always prefer the test statistic with the highest power.
For a two-tailed z-test at α = 0.05, critical values are ±1.96; at α = 0.01, critical values are ±2.576.
Test statistic formula: (sample statistic − hypothesized value) / standard error of the sample statistic.
Decreasing α (e.g., from 5% to 1%) reduces Type I error risk but increases Type II error risk (inverse relationship between the two error types).
Increasing sample size reduces the standard error, which increases test power and can reduce both types of errors simultaneously.

readings.title: Parametric And Non-Parametric Tests Of Independence
readings.summary:
This reading covers how to test whether two variables are statistically related, using both parametric and nonparametric methods. The core parametric test asks whether a population correlation coefficient (ρ) equals zero. When both variables are normally distributed, we use a t-statistic calculated as r√(n−2)/√(1−r²), which follows a t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom. A significant result means we reject the null that ρ = 0 and conclude the variables are correlated.

When data are not normally distributed or we only have ranked data, the Spearman rank correlation (rs) is the appropriate nonparametric alternative. It is computed from the differences in paired ranks: rs = 1 − (6Σdi²)/(n(n²−1)). Ties are handled by averaging the tied ranks. The significance of rs is tested using the same t-statistic formula as the parametric test, and for samples larger than 30, the statistic follows a t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom.

For categorical data, the chi-square test of independence uses a contingency (two-way) table. Each cell contains the observed count of items sharing two characteristics. Expected frequencies are computed as (row total × column total) / overall total, representing what we would see if the two characteristics were truly independent. The chi-square statistic sums (O−E)²/E across all cells.

The degrees of freedom for the chi-square test are (r−1)(c−1), where r and c are the number of row and column categories. If the computed chi-square statistic exceeds the critical value at the chosen significance level, we reject the hypothesis of independence and conclude the two characteristics are related. This test is widely applicable to practical finance questions such as whether earnings growth and dividend yield categories co-move.
key_points: Parametric correlation test: t = r√(n−2)/√(1−r²), t-distribution with n−2 degrees of freedom (NOT n−1).
The t-statistic for correlation increases with both larger r and larger sample size n.
Spearman rank correlation formula: rs = 1 − (6Σdi²)/(n(n²−1)); use when data are ranked or non-normal.
Spearman rank correlation significance is tested with the same t-statistic and n−2 df (valid for n > 30).
Contingency table chi-square test: χ² = Σ(O−E)²/E; degrees of freedom = (r−1)(c−1).
Expected cell frequency = (row total × column total) / grand total; reflects independence assumption.
Rejecting the chi-square null means the two categorical characteristics are NOT independent (i.e., they are related).

readings.title: Simple Linear Regression Module 10.1: Linear Regression
readings.summary:
Simple linear regression explains the variation in a dependent variable (also called the explained, endogenous, or predicted variable) using a single independent variable (also called the explanatory, exogenous, or predicting variable). The goal is to find a straight line — called the regression line or line of best fit — that best captures the linear relationship between the two variables as observed in a scatter plot of the data.

The regression line is estimated using the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) criterion, which minimizes the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) — the sum of the squared vertical distances between each actual Y-value and the corresponding predicted Y-value from the regression equation. This is why the method is called 'least squares.' The estimated regression equation takes the form Ŷ = b̂₀ + b̂₁X, where b̂₁ is the slope and b̂₀ is the intercept.

The slope coefficient (b̂₁) measures the expected change in the dependent variable for a one-unit change in the independent variable. It is calculated as the covariance of X and Y divided by the variance of X. The intercept (b̂₀) represents the estimated value of the dependent variable when the independent variable equals zero, and is computed as the mean of Y minus the slope times the mean of X. A key property of OLS is that the regression line always passes through the point (X̄, Ȳ).

Interpreting regression coefficients correctly is essential for the exam. For example, in a regression of ABC stock excess returns on S&P 500 excess returns, a slope of 0.64 means ABC excess returns are expected to change by 0.64% for every 1% change in S&P 500 excess returns. This slope coefficient in a market model is known as beta — a measure of systematic risk. A beta less than 1 indicates below-average market risk; greater than 1 indicates above-average risk.

Importantly, the magnitude of the slope coefficient alone does not indicate the strength of the relationship. Statistical significance must be assessed through hypothesis testing or confidence intervals before concluding that the independent variable meaningfully explains variation in the dependent variable.
key_points: Dependent variable = explained/endogenous/predicted; Independent variable = explanatory/exogenous/predicting — know all synonyms.
OLS minimizes the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE); the resulting estimates are called least squares estimates and the method is also called ordinary least squares (OLS) regression.
Slope formula: b̂₁ = Cov(X,Y) / Var(X); Intercept formula: b̂₀ = Ȳ − b̂₁X̄. The regression line always passes through (X̄, Ȳ).
Slope interpretation: expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X. Intercept interpretation: expected value of Y when X = 0.

In a market model regression (excess stock return on excess market return), the slope coefficient equals the stock's beta — a measure of systematic risk (beta < 1 = below-average risk, beta > 1 = above-average risk).
A large or small slope coefficient does NOT indicate a strong or weak relationship — statistical significance (hypothesis test or confidence interval) must be evaluated separately.
Residual (error) = Actual Y − Predicted Ŷ. The regression line minimizes the sum of these squared residuals, not the residuals themselves.

readings.title: Introduction To Big Data Techniques
readings.summary:
Fintech refers to technology developments applied to financial services, covering everything from handling large datasets to AI-driven analytics. Fintech companies focus on expanding data processing capabilities and building tools to extract actionable insights from vast amounts of information generated across the modern economy.

Big Data encompasses all potentially useful information generated in the economy, including traditional sources (financial markets, company reports, government statistics) and alternative sources such as social media posts, corporate exhaust (bank records, retail scanner data), and IoT sensor data. Big Data is characterized by the three Vs: Volume (growing from gigabytes to petabytes), Velocity (speed of data communication, with real-time feeds being low latency and periodic updates being high latency), and Variety (ranging from structured spreadsheets to semi-structured web code to unstructured video).

Data science extracts meaning from Big Data through five processing methods—Capture, Curation, Storage, Search, and Transfer—and through visualization techniques like charts, word clouds, and mind maps. Key challenges include ensuring data quality (handling outliers, missing data, and sampling bias) and organizing qualitative or unstructured data, which is where artificial intelligence becomes valuable. Neural networks simulate human brain processing and form a core AI tool.

Machine learning is a critical AI development in which algorithms learn patterns from data without human guidance or distributional assumptions. It follows a three-dataset workflow: training (find relationships), validation (refine models), and test (evaluate predictive power). Supervised learning uses labeled inputs and outputs; unsupervised learning uses unlabeled inputs to find structure; deep learning uses layered neural networks for increasingly complex pattern recognition. Key pitfalls are overfitting (model too complex, learns noise) and underfitting (model too simple, misses true patterns), plus the 'black box' problem of unexplainable outcomes.

Fintech applications in investment management include text analytics (analyzing unstructured text/voice, e.g., regulatory filings), natural language processing (interpreting human language for compliance checks or sentiment analysis of research reports), risk governance (using machine learning and real-time data for stress testing and risk modeling), and algorithmic trading (rule-based computerized trading for optimal execution, large order splitting across exchanges, and high-frequency trading).
key_points: Big Data has three defining characteristics — the 3 Vs: Volume (petabytes scale), Velocity (low latency = real-time; high latency = periodic), and Variety (structured → semi-structured → unstructured).
Alternative data sources include individual-generated data (social media, emails), corporate exhaust (bank/scanner records), and IoT sensors — all nontraditional inputs for financial analysis.
Data science processing steps: Capture → Curation (quality control) → Storage → Search → Transfer. Curation addresses outliers, bad/missing data, and sampling bias.
Machine learning workflow: Train on training dataset → Refine on validation dataset → Test predictive ability on test dataset. No assumptions about probability distributions.
Supervised learning uses labeled input/output data; unsupervised learning uses unlabeled input data to find structure; deep learning uses layered neural networks and can use either approach.
Overfitting = model too complex, treats noise as signal (spurious patterns); Underfitting = model too simple, misses true patterns. Both are machine learning failure modes to recognize.

Four fintech investment management applications: Text analytics (unstructured data analysis), Natural language processing (human language interpretation), Risk governance (ML-driven stress testing), and Algorithmic trading (rule-based execution, high-frequency trading).

readings.title: Firms And Market Structures Module 12.1: Breakeven, Shutdown, and Scale
readings.summary:
This reading distinguishes between the short run and long run for firms. In the short run, at least one factor of production (typically capital) is fixed, meaning a firm cannot avoid certain costs even if it shuts down. In the long run, all costs become variable and the firm can exit entirely. This distinction drives the different shutdown rules that apply in each time horizon.

For a price-taker firm under perfect competition, the breakeven point occurs where price (average revenue) equals average total cost, yielding zero economic profit. The short-run shutdown point is where price falls below average variable cost (AR < AVC): at this level, losses from operating exceed total fixed costs, so shutting down minimizes losses. Between AVC and ATC, the firm has losses but should continue operating short-run because it still covers variable costs and contributes toward fixed costs. In the long run, the firm shuts down if price remains below minimum ATC.

For price-searcher firms under imperfect competition, the same logic applies but is better analyzed using total revenue (TR) versus total cost (TC) and total variable cost (TVC), since marginal revenue no longer equals price. The conditions are: TR = TC means break even; TC > TR > TVC means operate short-run but exit long-run; TR < TVC means shut down immediately in both the short and long run.

The reading also covers economies and diseconomies of scale, analyzed through the long-run average total cost (LRATC) curve. This U-shaped curve envelopes all short-run ATC curves for different plant sizes. When LRATC falls as output increases, the firm enjoys economies of scale; when LRATC rises, diseconomies of scale set in. The minimum point of the LRATC curve is the minimum efficient scale. Under perfect competition, long-run equilibrium forces firms to operate at this minimum efficient scale, where LRATC equals market price and economic profit equals zero.
key_points: Short-run shutdown rule: Shut down if AR < AVC (losses exceed fixed costs); continue operating if AR > AVC even with losses, because you cover at least some fixed costs.
Long-run shutdown rule: Exit the market if AR < ATC (price cannot cover all costs); stay if AR ≥ ATC.
Breakeven point: AR = ATC, meaning TR = TC and economic profit = zero (not accounting profit).
For imperfect competition (price-searchers), use total revenue vs. total cost: TR < TVC → shut down now; TVC < TR < TC → operate short-run only; TR ≥ TC → stay in market.
The LRATC curve is U-shaped and envelopes all SRATC curves; its minimum point is the minimum efficient scale where long-run per-unit costs are lowest.
Economies of scale: LRATC falls as output/scale increases. Diseconomies of scale: LRATC rises as output/scale increases (often due to management complexity).
Under perfect competition, long-run equilibrium requires firms operate at minimum efficient scale with zero economic profit and P = LRATC = minimum ATC.

readings.title: Understanding Business Cycles
readings.summary:
The business cycle describes recurring fluctuations in economic activity measured by real GDP. It has four phases: expansion (rising GDP), peak (GDP stops rising), contraction/recession (falling GDP), and trough (GDP stops falling). Economists typically define a recession as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP, though agencies like the NBER use broader data including unemployment, industrial production, and inflation to identify turning points. Three ways to measure cycles exist: the classical cycle (GDP vs. a base value), the growth cycle (GDP vs. long-term trend), and the growth rate cycle (annualized growth rate changes) — the last being preferred by practitioners.

Credit cycles refer to cyclical swings in the availability and cost of credit. During expansions, lenders loosen standards and lower rates; during contractions, credit tightens. Credit cycles can amplify business cycles — loose credit during expansions may inflate asset bubbles (e.g., subprime mortgages pre-2008), making expansions stronger and recessions deeper. Importantly, credit cycles and business cycles do not always coincide, and credit cycles tend to be longer in duration.

Inventories and labor are key indicators of cycle turning points. Near a peak, slowing sales cause unplanned inventory accumulation (rising inventory-sales ratio), prompting firms to cut production — a leading signal of contraction. Near a trough, depleted inventories signal recovery as firms ramp production. Firms adjust labor gradually: they first change hours and overtime before hiring or laying off, and similarly use existing capital more or less intensively before investing in or disposing of plant and equipment.

Consumer spending, the largest GDP component, is cyclically sensitive but varies by category. Durable goods spending (appliances, autos, furniture) is highly cyclical because consumers defer big-ticket purchases during uncertainty. Services spending is moderately cyclical — discretionary services (travel, dining) fluctuate more than necessities (healthcare, insurance). Nondurable goods (food, household products) are relatively stable across the cycle.

Housing and external trade sectors also follow cyclical patterns. Housing is sensitive to mortgage rates and consumer confidence, often leading the broader cycle. External trade is influenced by the relative strength of domestic vs. foreign economies and currency movements — a stronger domestic economy typically widens the trade deficit as imports rise. Economic indicators are classified as leading (predict turning points), coincident (move with GDP), or lagging (confirm trends after the fact), and analysts use composite indexes of these to track and forecast cycle phases.
key_points: Four business cycle phases in order: Expansion → Peak → Contraction/Recession → Trough; a recession is conventionally defined as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP.

Three cycle measurement methods: classical cycle (vs. base GDP), growth cycle (vs. trend GDP), and growth rate cycle (annualized growth rate changes) — the growth rate cycle is preferred by economists and shows peaks/troughs earliest.

Credit cycles amplify business cycles: loose credit fuels expansions and asset bubbles; tight credit deepens recessions. Credit cycles are generally longer in duration than business cycles and do not always coincide with them.
Rising inventory-sales ratio above normal → leading warning sign of an approaching peak/contraction; falling ratio below normal → early signal of recovery/expansion.
Firms adjust labor and capital gradually: hours/overtime are changed first, then hiring/layoffs; existing capital is used more/less intensively before new investment or equipment sales.
Consumer spending cyclicality by category (most to least): Durable goods (most cyclical) > Discretionary services > Non-discretionary services > Nondurable goods (most stable).

Economic indicators are classified as leading (e.g., stock prices, building permits), coincident (e.g., industrial production, personal income), or lagging (e.g., unemployment rate, CPI) — leading indicators are most useful for predicting cycle turning points.

readings.title: Fiscal Policy Module 14.1: Fiscal Policy
readings.summary:
Fiscal policy is a government's use of spending and taxation to influence economic activity, while monetary policy is the central bank's management of money and credit. A budget surplus occurs when revenues exceed expenditures; a deficit occurs when expenditures exceed revenues. Expanding the deficit (more spending or less taxes) is expansionary and tends to boost GDP; shrinking the deficit is contractionary. Both tools are used to pursue stable prices and positive growth, but only fiscal policy is explicitly used for redistribution of income and wealth.

Keynesian economists believe fiscal policy strongly affects aggregate demand and is especially powerful when the economy is below full employment. Monetarists, by contrast, view fiscal stimulus as only temporary and prefer monetary policy to manage inflation over time — but do not advocate using monetary policy to smooth business cycles. Discretionary fiscal policy involves deliberate government decisions on spending and taxes, while automatic stabilizers (e.g., unemployment insurance rising in recessions, tax revenues falling) operate without explicit legislative action and naturally cushion economic fluctuations.

When governments run persistent deficits, they accumulate debt. A key metric is the debt-to-GDP ratio. If the real interest rate on government debt exceeds the real GDP growth rate, the debt ratio rises over time — a potential solvency concern. If real growth exceeds the real interest rate, the debt ratio naturally declines even without primary surpluses.

Arguments for concern about large deficits include: higher future taxes discouraging work and investment, loss of market confidence triggering default or inflationary money-printing, and the crowding-out effect where government borrowing raises interest rates and displaces private investment. Arguments against concern include: domestically-held debt is less problematic, debt financing productive investment can pay for itself, Ricardian equivalence (private savings offset the deficit), and deficits below full employment do not divert capital from productive uses.

Fiscal policy tools include spending tools (transfer payments, current spending, capital spending) and revenue tools (direct taxes on income/wealth and indirect taxes on goods/services). Desirable tax policy attributes are simplicity, efficiency (minimizing distortions), fairness, and revenue sufficiency. Capital spending on infrastructure is particularly valued for boosting long-run productivity.
key_points: Budget deficit = expansionary (raises GDP/demand); budget surplus = contractionary — memorize the direction for both fiscal spending changes AND tax changes.
Keynesians: fiscal policy is powerful at less-than-full employment. Monetarists: fiscal stimulus is only temporary; use monetary policy for inflation, not to counter business cycles.
Automatic stabilizers (unemployment benefits, tax revenues) act counter-cyclically without legislation — they increase deficits in recessions and reduce them in booms.
Debt ratio rises if real interest rate on debt > real GDP growth rate; debt ratio falls if real GDP growth > real interest rate on debt.
Crowding-out effect: government borrowing raises interest rates → reduces private investment → partially offsets the expansionary impact of deficit spending.
Ricardian equivalence argues deficits don't matter because households save in anticipation of future tax hikes, exactly offsetting the fiscal stimulus.
Transfer payments (Social Security, unemployment insurance) are NOT included in GDP; capital spending on infrastructure IS included and boosts future productivity.

readings.title: Monetary Policy Module 15.1: Central Bank
readings.summary:
Central banks serve six core roles in an economy: they are the sole supplier of currency (fiat money), act as banker to the government and commercial banks, regulate and supervise the payments system, serve as lender of last resort to prevent bank runs, hold gold and foreign exchange reserves, and conduct monetary policy. The shift from gold-backed currency to fiat money means modern currency holds value through legal mandate and public trust rather than any tangible backing.

The primary objective of most central banks is price stability through inflation control. High inflation imposes real costs: menu costs (businesses must constantly reprice goods) and shoe leather costs (individuals make frequent bank trips to avoid holding depreciating cash). Most developed countries target inflation in the 2–3% range; a zero target is deliberately avoided because it risks deflation, which can be equally disruptive. The U.S. Fed is notable for having a dual mandate — price stability plus maximum employment — and does not publish an explicit inflation target.

Central banks implement monetary policy through three main tools. First, the policy rate (discount rate in the U.S., refinancing rate at the ECB, two-week repo rate at the Bank of England) controls the cost of borrowing for commercial banks, which ripples into broader lending rates. Second, reserve requirements set the floor on what banks must hold, directly constraining the money multiplier. Third, open market operations — buying or selling securities — are the most frequently used tool in the U.S., directly affecting bank reserves and the federal funds rate.

The monetary transmission mechanism describes how a policy rate change flows through the economy to affect inflation. A rate increase (contractionary policy) raises short-term lending rates, dampening consumer credit and business investment; lowers bond, equity, and asset prices (creating a negative wealth effect that reduces consumption); shifts expectations toward slower growth; and attracts foreign capital, appreciating the domestic currency and reducing import prices. These four channels — short-term rates, asset values, expectations, and exchange rates — collectively transmit policy intent to the price level. Exchange rate pegging is a related tool some countries use, but it forces the pegging country to mirror the inflation rate and monetary conditions of the anchor country.
key_points: Memorize the 6 roles of central banks: currency supplier, government banker, payments regulator, lender of last resort, FX reserve holder, monetary policy conductor.
Primary CB objective = price stability (inflation control); target is ~2–3%, NOT zero (zero risks deflation).
U.S. Fed has a DUAL mandate (price stability + maximum employment) and no explicit inflation target — a common exam trap.
Three monetary policy tools: (1) Policy rate — discount rate (Fed), refinancing rate (ECB), repo rate (BoE); (2) Reserve requirements; (3) Open market operations (most used by the Fed).
Open market operations: CB BUYS securities → money supply increases → interest rates fall (expansionary); CB SELLS → money supply falls → rates rise (contractionary).
Four channels of the monetary transmission mechanism: short-term interest rates, asset prices/wealth effect, inflation/growth expectations, and exchange rates.
Exchange rate pegging: a country that pegs its currency to the USD essentially imports the anchor country's inflation rate and loses independent monetary control.

readings.title: Introduction To Geopolitics Module 16.1: Geopolitics
readings.summary:
Geopolitics examines interactions among nations through both state actors (national governments) and nonstate actors (corporations, NGOs, individuals), and how geography shapes those interactions. A central lens for analysis is the cooperation-versus-noncooperation spectrum: countries cooperate when doing so advances their national interests, which can be thought of as a hierarchy with survival at the top. A country's geophysical endowments, political cycle, and cultural institutions all influence where it falls on that spectrum at any given time.

Globalization — the long-term trend toward worldwide economic and cultural integration — is contrasted with nationalism, meaning a country pursuing its economic interests independently of others. Using a two-axis framework (cooperation vs. noncooperation, and globalization vs. nationalism), four geopolitical archetypes emerge: Autarky (noncooperative + nationalist), Hegemony (noncooperative + globalist), Bilateralism (cooperative + nationalist), and Multilateralism (cooperative + globalist). Countries rarely fit neatly into one quadrant; their positions shift over time with domestic politics and external pressures.

Nonstate actors — businesses and investors — often push toward globalization even when their governments do not, seeking profits, cost savings, and diversification through portfolio investment flows and foreign direct investment. Cultural factors like shared language, emigration history, and strong institutions (e.g., intellectual property protection) also drive cooperation and can confer soft power — the ability to influence other countries without force.

Three major international organizations facilitate global trade and cooperation. The IMF focuses on monetary cooperation, exchange rate stability, and providing balance-of-payments support to member countries. The World Bank provides financial and technical assistance to developing nations to reduce poverty and build capacity. The WTO administers trade agreements, serves as a forum for negotiations, and handles trade disputes, working to lower trade barriers and ensure non-discriminatory treatment among members. Together these institutions form the institutional backbone of the multilateral trading system.
key_points: Geopolitics involves both STATE actors (governments) and NONSTATE actors (corporations, NGOs, individuals); geography is a core influence on interactions.
Countries cooperate when it advances their NATIONAL INTERESTS, which form a hierarchy with survival (security) at the top — resource endowments shape those interests.

Know the 4 archetypes on the cooperation/globalization matrix: AUTARKY (noncooperative + nationalist), HEGEMONY (noncooperative + globalist), BILATERALISM (cooperative + nationalist), MULTILATERALISM (cooperative + globalist).
SOFT POWER = ability to influence other countries without force or threat of force; cultural exports and strong institutions (e.g., IP protection) are key enablers.
IMF goals: monetary cooperation, exchange rate stability, balanced trade growth, multilateral payments system, and balance-of-payments assistance to members.
WORLD BANK: provides financial and technical assistance to DEVELOPING countries to fight poverty — distinct from the IMF's monetary/stability focus.
WTO: administers trade agreements, provides dispute resolution, and works to lower trade barriers on a non-discriminatory basis among member countries.

readings.title: International Trade Module 17.1: International Trade
readings.summary:
International trade generates gains for participating countries primarily through comparative advantage — when countries specialize in producing goods at which they have a lower relative cost and trade for the rest, total global output rises and both nations benefit. Newer trade models add further justifications: economies of scale that lower export costs, greater product variety for consumers, increased competition that improves quality and reduces prices, and more efficient allocation of productive resources. Even countries producing the same type of good can benefit through differentiated products (monopolistic competition), as seen in the global auto market where countries simultaneously export and import different vehicle types.

Despite these broad gains, free trade also imposes real costs. The most significant are job losses in import-competing domestic industries and increased income inequality. For example, a high-labor-cost country that imports textiles from cheaper producers will see wage and employment declines in that sector. Importantly, in the exporting country, rising production costs can hurt domestic consumers. Economists generally argue that in aggregate the gains exceed the losses — in theory, winners could compensate losers — but in practice the short-run disruptions to specific workers, industries, and regions are significant even if mitigated over the long run.

Governments impose trade restrictions for various stated reasons. Economically defensible justifications include protecting infant industries (allowing new sectors to achieve scale before facing global competition) and national security (ensuring domestic supply of strategically critical goods). Less defensible arguments include protecting jobs broadly, shielding politically connected industries, and retaliating against foreign restrictions. Common trade restriction tools are tariffs (taxes on imports), quotas (quantity limits), export subsidies (payments to domestic exporters), minimum domestic content requirements, and voluntary export restraints (VERs).

The economic effects of tariffs and quotas are largely symmetric but differ in one key area: who captures the revenue. A tariff raises the domestic price, reduces import volume, increases domestic production, and transfers revenue to the domestic government. A quota achieves the same price and quantity effects but the revenue (quota rents) may go to foreign exporters holding import licenses rather than the domestic government — unless the government auctions those licenses. In both cases there is a deadweight loss representing the net welfare cost to the domestic economy. Export subsidies lower the price for foreign buyers, benefiting foreign consumers while domestic consumers pay higher prices, and they also create deadweight loss.
key_points: Comparative advantage drives classical trade gains: countries specialize where relative costs are lowest, increasing total output for both trading partners.
Newer trade models add gains from economies of scale, product variety, increased competition, and efficient resource reallocation — not just comparative advantage.
Costs of free trade: short-run job losses and income inequality in import-competing industries; in the long run, workers retrain and costs are mitigated.
Economically valid reasons for trade restrictions: infant industry protection and national security. Protecting domestic jobs/industries broadly has little theoretical support.

Tariff vs. quota equivalence: an 'equivalent quota' produces the same domestic price and import volume as a tariff, but tariff revenue goes to the domestic government while quota rents may go to foreign exporters (unless licenses are auctioned).
Deadweight loss exists under both tariffs and quotas, representing the net welfare cost to the domestic economy beyond what is transferred to producers or government.
Export subsidies benefit foreign consumers (lower prices) but raise domestic consumer prices and generate deadweight loss — the domestic government bears the subsidy cost.

readings.title: Capital Flows And The Fx Market
readings.summary: The foreign exchange (FX) market exists primarily to facilitate international trade and capital flows. Companies and individuals buy foreign currencies to purchase goods, services, and financial assets denominated in those currencies. Participants include the sell side (large multinational banks acting as primary dealers) and the buy side (corporations, investment accounts, governments, central banks, and retail customers). Hedging refers to taking an FX position to reduce existing currency risk, while speculating refers to taking a position that increases currency risk.
key_points: Exchange rate notation: USD/EUR means USD is the price currency and EUR is the base currency — the quote shows the cost of one unit of the base in terms of the price currency.
Direct quote vs. indirect quote: A USD/EUR quote is a direct quote for a USD-based investor (price is in their home currency) and an indirect quote for a EUR-based investor.
Nominal exchange rate is the current spot price; if USD/EUR rises, the USD weakens (costs more USD to buy one EUR).

Real exchange rate adjusts the nominal rate for relative price level changes: Real P/B = Nominal P/B × (CPI_price / CPI_base). Higher foreign (base country) inflation raises the real exchange rate, reducing USD purchasing power in that country.
Hedging reduces FX risk (e.g., a forward contract to lock in a future exchange rate); speculating increases FX risk by taking an open currency position.

Buy-side participants: corporations (hedging trade flows), real money accounts (mutual funds, pensions — no derivatives), leveraged accounts (hedge funds — use derivatives), governments/sovereign wealth funds, and retail FX traders.
Percentage change in a currency: % change = (new rate − old rate) / old rate, applied to the base currency; if USD/EUR rises from 1.10 to 1.15, the EUR appreciated by ~4.5% and the USD depreciated.

readings.title: Exchange Rate Calculations Module 19.1: Foreign Exchange
readings.summary: This reading introduces the mechanics of foreign exchange rates, starting with cross-rate calculations. A cross rate is the exchange rate between two currencies derived from their individual exchange rates against a common third currency (typically USD or EUR). The key technique is to set up the quotations algebraically so that units cancel correctly — for example, (CHF/USD) ÷ (NZD/USD) gives CHF/NZD. If a rate is given in the wrong direction, take its inverse before multiplying or dividing.
key_points: Cross-rate formula: arrange currency pairs so units cancel algebraically. (A/B) × (B/C) = A/C, or (A/B) ÷ (C/B) = A/C. Take the inverse of any quote that is in the wrong direction.
No-arbitrage forward rate formula: F = S × [(1 + r_price) / (1 + r_base)], where r_price is the interest rate of the price currency and r_base is the interest rate of the base currency.
The currency with the HIGHER interest rate must DEPRECIATE in the forward market by approximately the interest rate differential — this is the core of interest rate parity.

For sub-annual forward rates (30, 90, 180 days), use the money market yield for that period directly in the formula — do NOT annualize or de-annualize by dividing by 12 or 4 separately; the rate is already quoted for that period.

Forward points: each point = 1 unit of the last decimal place of the spot quote. Add (positive points) or subtract (negative points) the point value directly to the spot rate. E.g., spot 2.3481 + 18.3 points → 2.3481 + 0.00183 = 2.34993.
Forward rate in percentage terms: F = S × (1 + % premium/discount). A negative percentage means the forward currency is at a discount relative to spot.

Arbitrage enforcement: if the forward rate deviates from no-arbitrage parity, traders borrow the low-rate currency, invest in the high-rate currency, and lock in the forward rate. This drives the spot and forward rates back to parity — so the no-arbitrage condition is self-correcting.

readings.title: Organizational Forms, Corporate Issuer Features, and Ownership
readings.summary: This reading covers the four main organizational forms businesses can take: sole proprietorships, general partnerships, limited partnerships, and corporations. Sole proprietorships and general partnerships are simple structures where owners have unlimited personal liability and profits are taxed as personal income. Limited partnerships add a layer of limited partners who enjoy liability capped at their investment, while general partners retain unlimited liability and manage operations. Corporations are distinct in that they are separate legal entities, granting all shareholders limited liability and enabling access to large amounts of both debt and equity capital — making them the dominant structure for large firms.
key_points: Unlimited liability applies to sole proprietors and general partners; limited partners and corporate shareholders have liability limited to their investment only.
Corporations are taxed as separate legal entities, creating potential double taxation: corporate profits taxed at the entity level, then dividends taxed again as personal income to shareholders.
Double taxation impact is reduced when a corporation retains (reinvests) earnings rather than paying them out as dividends — lower payout ratio = lower effective tax burden.

Public companies must meet compliance and reporting requirements (quarterly/annual filings, material disclosures); private companies face fewer regulatory obligations and can take a longer-term business view.
Free float = shares actively traded by the public (excluding insider, strategic, or sponsor holdings), expressed as a percentage of total shares outstanding.

Private companies can go public via three routes: IPO (issuing new shares on an exchange), direct listing (existing shares listed without new issuance), or acquisition by a special purpose acquisition company (SPAC).
Private placements allow private companies to raise equity capital, but are restricted to accredited investors (institutions, high-net-worth individuals) due to limited regulatory oversight.

readings.title: Investors And Other Stakeholders
readings.summary:
Reading 23 begins by distinguishing the financial claims of lenders and shareholders. Lenders hold a contractual, senior claim to interest and principal payments, while equity holders have only a residual claim to net assets after all obligations are met. Because debt is less risky, it is cheaper capital. Debt investors have limited upside (capped at promised payments) but share downside exposure if firm value falls below the debt's face value, whereas equity has theoretically unlimited upside alongside full downside risk. Leverage amplifies ROE when returns on assets exceed the cost of debt, but magnifies losses when they do not.

Stakeholder theory broadens the focus of corporate governance beyond just shareholders to include lenders, the board of directors, senior managers, employees, customers, suppliers, and society at large. Each group has distinct interests: shareholders want growth and profitability; lenders want repayment certainty and use covenants (e.g., maximum leverage, minimum interest coverage ratios) to limit borrower risk-taking; the board oversees management and protects shareholder interests; senior managers seek job security and compensation maximization. Conflicts can arise, particularly between equity holders (who may favor risky growth strategies) and debtholders (who bear downside risk without sharing in the upside).

Board structure matters significantly. A one-tier board includes both inside directors (executives, founders) and independent directors on a single board; a two-tier structure (common in continental Europe) separates a supervisory board of independent directors from a management board of insiders. Staggered boards—where only a fraction of directors stand for election each year—reduce shareholder ability to rapidly replace the board, providing continuity but limiting accountability. Major exchanges require that most directors be independent to protect shareholders.

Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) factors have grown increasingly important to investors and companies. ESG considerations can affect a firm's long-term value, risk profile, and relationships with all stakeholder groups. Institutional investors and regulators increasingly demand ESG disclosures and integrate ESG analysis into investment decisions. Companies that manage ESG risks effectively may enjoy lower costs of capital and stronger stakeholder relationships, while those that neglect them face reputational, regulatory, and financial risks.
key_points: Lenders have a senior, contractual claim (interest + principal); equity holders have a residual claim — debt is higher priority and lower cost than equity.
Debt investors have NO upside beyond promised payments but share downside if firm value < debt value; equity has unlimited upside but bears full downside.
Leverage increases ROE when return on assets > cost of debt; covenants (max leverage, min interest coverage) protect debtholders from equity holders taking on excess risk.
Shareholder theory focuses governance on maximizing equity value; stakeholder theory broadens focus to lenders, employees, customers, suppliers, and society.
One-tier boards (inside + independent directors on one board) vs. two-tier boards (supervisory board of independents overseeing a management board) — two-tier is common in continental Europe.
Staggered boards elect only a fraction of directors per year, reducing shareholder power to overhaul the board quickly but providing strategic continuity.
ESG factors (Environmental, Social, Governance) are material to long-term firm value and risk; poor ESG management can increase cost of capital and create regulatory/reputational risk.

readings.title: Corporate Governance: Conflicts, Mechanisms, Risks,
readings.summary:
Corporate governance addresses the principal-agent problem that arises when one party (the principal) hires another (the agent) to act on their behalf. In a corporation, shareholders are the principals and managers/directors are the agents. Because their interests diverge—managers face concentrated employment risk while shareholders can diversify—managers may take insufficient risk, pursue empire-building acquisitions, entrench themselves, or engage in self-dealing. Information asymmetry worsens these conflicts, especially at large, complex companies with low institutional ownership.

Conflicts also arise between different shareholder groups. Controlling shareholders with concentrated wealth in the firm may push for value-destroying diversification that minority shareholders—who already hold diversified portfolios—do not want. Dual-class share structures can entrench a founder group with voting power disproportionate to their economic interest, which CFA Institute explicitly opposes. Meanwhile, shareholders and creditors conflict over risk appetite: shareholders benefit from upside while creditors bear default risk, so shareholders may prefer riskier strategies, higher dividends, or additional debt issuance that harms existing debtholders.

Corporate governance mechanisms exist to manage and minimize these conflicts. Key tools include annual general meetings (AGMs) where shareholders vote on ordinary resolutions (simple majority) and special resolutions (supermajority, e.g., 75%), proxy voting, board oversight, audits, and transparent financial reporting. The board of directors—particularly independent/non-executive directors—plays a central role in monitoring management. Board committees (audit, remuneration, nominations) provide specialized oversight and should be composed primarily or entirely of independent directors.

Beyond internal mechanisms, external forces also discipline corporate behavior. These include the market for corporate control (takeover threat disciplines underperforming managers), securities regulators, creditors imposing debt covenants, and activist shareholders or proxy advisors. Well-governed companies tend to achieve lower cost of capital, better access to financing, and stronger long-term performance. Poor governance raises operational, legal, reputational, and financial risks, and can lead to outright corporate failure.
key_points: Principal-agent conflict: shareholders (principals) vs. managers/directors (agents) — managers may under-risk, empire-build, self-deal, or entrench; information asymmetry amplifies the problem.

Controlling vs. minority shareholder conflict: controlling shareholders may push for diversification that destroys value for already-diversified minority holders; dual-class structures (opposed by CFA Institute) can give founders outsized voting power.

Shareholder vs. creditor conflict: shareholders prefer more risk (limited downside), creditors prefer less; issuing new debt, raising dividends, or increasing leverage harms existing debtholders most — especially long-term debtholders.
Ordinary resolutions require a simple majority (>50%); special resolutions (e.g., amending articles, major transactions) require a supermajority (typically 75%) — know the distinction for the exam.

Board independence is critical: independent (non-executive) directors should dominate key committees (audit, remuneration, nominations) to reduce conflicts; inside directors risk favoring management over shareholders.

Corporate governance mechanisms: AGMs, proxy voting, board oversight, external audits, securities regulation, market for corporate control (takeover threat), creditor covenants, and activist shareholders all serve to align agent behavior with principal interests.
Benefits of strong governance: lower cost of capital, better access to financing, higher valuations, and reduced operational/legal/reputational risk — poor governance can lead to corporate failure.

readings.title: Working Capital And Liquidity
readings.summary:
The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures how efficiently a company converts its investments in inventory and receivables into cash. It is calculated as Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH) plus Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) minus Days Payable Outstanding (DPO). A lower CCC is preferable because it means less capital is tied up in working capital. Companies can shorten the CCC by reducing inventory, tightening credit to customers, or extending supplier payment terms — though each carries trade-offs such as supply disruptions, lost sales, or strained supplier relationships.

When a company forgoes an early-payment discount from a supplier, it is effectively borrowing from the supplier at an implicit cost. This cost is calculated as EAR = (1 + discount/(1 - discount))^(365/(net days - discount days)) - 1. This rate is often very high (e.g., 2/10 net 30 implies ~44.6% EAR), so companies typically prefer explicit bank financing. CCC varies significantly by industry — airlines have low CCCs due to prepaid sales and minimal inventory, while pharmaceutical companies have high CCCs due to large inventory buffers.

Liquidity for a corporate issuer refers to the availability of cash and liquid assets to meet short-term obligations. Primary liquidity sources include cash on hand, marketable securities, bank credit lines, and operating cash flows. Secondary sources — such as suspending dividends, selling assets, issuing equity, or restructuring debt — are costlier and send negative signals to the market. Drags on liquidity (slow receivables, excess inventory) and pulls on liquidity (accelerated payables, reduced supplier credit) can increase the CCC and strain liquidity.

Analysts use three key liquidity ratios to evaluate a firm's short-term financial health. The current ratio (current assets / current liabilities) is the broadest measure. The quick ratio excludes inventory, which is the least liquid current asset. The cash ratio is the most stringent, including only cash and marketable securities. These ratios should be compared within the same industry and tracked over time, as acceptable levels vary considerably by sector.
key_points: CCC formula: DOH + DSO - DPO. Lower CCC = better liquidity efficiency; analysts compare CCC within the same industry or over time for one company.
EAR of supplier financing = (1 + a/(1-a))^(365/(c-b)) - 1. The implicit cost of forgoing a supplier discount is usually much higher than bank borrowing rates — prefer explicit financing.

Primary liquidity sources (cash, marketable securities, bank lines, operating cash flow) are preferred; secondary sources (asset sales, equity issuance, debt restructuring, dividend suspension) signal financial distress and are more costly.
Drag on liquidity = cash inflows lag (rising DSO or DOH). Pull on liquidity = cash outflows accelerate (falling DPO). Both increase the CCC and reduce liquidity.

Three liquidity ratios in order of stringency: Current ratio (CA/CL) > Quick ratio ((CA - Inventory)/CL) > Cash ratio (Cash + Marketable Securities)/CL). Inventory is excluded from quick ratio because it is the least liquid current asset.
Net working capital = operating current assets - operating current liabilities (excludes non-operating items like excess cash or short-term debt), and is closely linked to the CCC.
Cost of liquidity = discount to fair value incurred when assets must be sold quickly to meet immediate cash needs; calculated as dollar discount divided by fair value.

readings.title: Capital Investments And Capital Allocation
readings.summary:
Capital investments fall into four categories: going concern projects (maintaining operations or reducing costs), regulatory/compliance projects (mandated by government or insurers, little to no revenue), expansion projects (entering new markets or products, requiring detailed demand forecasting), and other projects (outside existing business lines, similar to startups or acquisitions). Going concern projects require minimal analysis, while expansion and other projects demand rigorous cash flow forecasting due to their complexity and uncertainty.

The capital allocation process is the framework for evaluating projects whose cash flows extend beyond one year. It follows four steps: idea generation, analyzing project proposals via cash flow forecasts, creating a firm-wide capital budget that aligns with strategy and resources, and monitoring/post-auditing decisions to identify forecasting errors and improve future estimates. Capital allocation is considered a manager's most critical responsibility because long-term asset decisions shape the firm's future and directly tie to shareholder value maximization.

NPV is the sum of present values of all incremental after-tax cash flows discounted at the firm's risk-adjusted cost of capital. A positive NPV increases shareholder wealth and should be accepted; negative NPV projects should be rejected. IRR is the discount rate that sets NPV to zero (PV inflows = PV outflows). The IRR decision rule: accept if IRR exceeds the required rate of return (hurdle rate), reject if it falls below. For independent projects, NPV and IRR generally agree on accept/reject decisions.

ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) measures after-tax operating profit relative to the total capital invested and is used to assess whether a firm creates value above its cost of capital. When NPV and IRR conflict—most commonly for mutually exclusive projects—NPV is the preferred method because it directly measures value creation in dollar terms and assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital, a more realistic assumption than IRR's implicit reinvestment at the IRR rate itself.
key_points: Four capital investment types: going concern (minimal analysis needed), regulatory/compliance (no revenue, evaluate alternatives), expansion (detailed forecasting required), and other/new business (highest uncertainty, startup-like risk).
Four steps of capital allocation: (1) idea generation, (2) cash flow analysis, (3) firm-wide budget prioritization, (4) post-audit monitoring — post-audit corrects systematic forecasting errors.
NPV rule: accept if NPV > 0 (adds shareholder value), reject if NPV < 0. Discount rate = firm's cost of capital adjusted for project risk.
IRR rule: accept if IRR > hurdle rate (required rate of return), reject if IRR < hurdle rate. IRR is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
When NPV and IRR conflict on mutually exclusive projects, always prefer NPV — it measures absolute value creation and uses the more realistic reinvestment-at-cost-of-capital assumption.
ROIC = after-tax operating profit / invested capital. A ROIC above the cost of capital indicates value creation; below indicates value destruction.
Match funding principle: finance projects with capital sources whose maturity is consistent with project life to reduce financing risk.

readings.title: Capital Structure Module 27.1: Weighted-Average
readings.summary:
The weighted-average cost of capital (WACC) is a blended rate reflecting a firm's cost of debt and equity, weighted by their proportions in the capital structure. The formula is: WACC = [weight of debt × pretax cost of debt × (1 − tax rate)] + (weight of equity × cost of equity). Debt carries a lower cost than equity due to its priority claim, and interest expense is tax-deductible, so the cost of debt is adjusted to an after-tax figure. Weights may be target weights or market value weights; market value weights better reflect current opportunity cost.

A firm's optimal capital structure minimizes WACC while matching financing duration to asset life. Factors affecting how much debt a company can support are classified as internal or external. Internal factors include business risk, revenue stability, cash flow predictability, asset type and liquidity, existing debt levels, and the corporate tax rate. External factors include macroeconomic conditions, business cycle stage, regulation, and industry norms.

Companies with stable, recurring revenues — such as those in noncyclical industries or with subscription-based models — can sustain higher debt ratios. Tangible, liquid, fungible assets serve as better collateral than intangible assets, improving debt access and reducing borrowing costs. Low operating leverage (low fixed costs) also supports higher debt capacity. Leverage ratios (debt-to-equity, debt-to-EBIT) and coverage ratios (interest coverage = EBIT / interest expense) are key tools for assessing debt capacity.

A firm's life cycle stage is central to its capital structure. Start-ups rely almost exclusively on equity due to negative or low cash flows, high business risk, and minimal collateral. Growth-stage firms begin using modest debt, often secured by receivables or fixed assets. Mature firms have stable cash flows and wide access to unsecured debt at low cost. Top-down macroeconomic factors — inflation, real GDP growth, monetary policy, exchange rates — also influence benchmark rates and credit spreads, especially during economic downturns when yield spreads widen for cyclical issuers.
key_points: WACC formula: WACC = [Wd × Kd × (1 − t)] + [We × Ke]; memorize the after-tax adjustment applied only to the cost of debt, not equity.
Use market value weights (not book value) when estimating the current opportunity cost of capital, as they reflect current market conditions.
Debt capacity drivers: stable/recurring revenues, predictable cash flows, low business risk, tangible/liquid assets, and low existing leverage all increase ability to issue debt.
Life cycle and debt: Start-up = equity only (high risk, no collateral); Growth = some secured debt; Mature = broad access to unsecured debt at low rates.
Interest coverage ratio = EBIT / Interest Expense; a key metric for assessing a firm's ability to service debt — higher is better.
Companies with low operating leverage (low fixed costs) and subscription-based revenues can support more debt than high-fixed-cost, cyclical, or pay-per-use businesses.
Top-down factors (inflation, GDP growth, monetary policy) affect benchmark rates and credit spreads; in downturns, spreads widen — especially for cyclical industries.

readings.title: Business Models Module 28.1: Business Model
readings.summary: A business model explains how a firm provides a product or service, finds customers, delivers value, and generates profit.
