The core framework answers five questions: who (customer segments and acquisition), how (key assets and suppliers), what (the product/service and its differentiation), where (channel strategy including direct vs. intermediary sales, and omnichannel approaches), and how much (pricing strategy). Firms selling to other businesses are B2B; firms selling to consumers are B2C.
key_points: Business model framework = Who (customers/segmenting), What (product/differentiation), How (assets/suppliers), Where (channel strategy), How Much (pricing)
Pricing strategies: commodity producers are price takers; differentiated products or few competitors = pricing power. Price discrimination includes tiered, dynamic, value-based, and auction pricing.
Multi-product pricing models: bundling, razors-and-blades (low-margin hardware + high-margin consumables), add-on pricing (high-margin options after purchase decision).
Other pricing models: penetration pricing (low price to gain share), freemium (basic free, premium paid), hidden revenue (free content funded by ads/data).
Alternatives to outright purchase: subscription model (e.g., Microsoft Office), licensing (biotech licensing a drug), and franchising (franchisee pays % of sales for brand/support).

Value proposition = how customers value the product vs. competition; value chain = how the firm executes that proposition. Porter's five value chain activities: inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing, sales and service.

Network effects: value of a network grows as user base grows (e.g., WhatsApp, eBay, Facebook); supports penetration pricing strategy. Two-sided/multi-sided networks (e.g., Airbnb) connect distinct user groups.

readings.title: Analyzing Income Statements Module 30.1: Revenue Recognition
readings.summary:
Revenue recognition follows the accrual principle: revenue is recognized when earned, not necessarily when cash is received. If goods are sold on credit, revenue is recognized at the time of sale with an accounts receivable created. Conversely, if cash is received before goods or services are delivered, a liability (unearned revenue) is recorded and converted to revenue only as the obligation is fulfilled — the magazine subscription being a classic example.

Both IFRS and U.S. GAAP have converged on a principles-based five-step framework: (1) identify the contract, (2) identify distinct performance obligations, (3) determine the transaction price, (4) allocate that price to each obligation, and (5) recognize revenue when each obligation is satisfied. A key nuance is that 'collectability must be probable' for a contract to exist, but IFRS and U.S. GAAP define 'probable' differently, which can lead to different accounting outcomes for the same transaction.

For long-term contracts, revenue is recognized based on progress toward completion, measured either from the input side (e.g., costs incurred as a percentage of total estimated costs) or the output side (e.g., engineering milestones). A performance obligation is satisfied over time when the customer receives ongoing benefit, the supplier creates an asset the customer controls during creation, or the asset has no alternative use and the supplier has enforceable payment rights. Costs to secure long-term contracts (e.g., sales commissions) must be capitalized and expensed over the contract life.

The agent vs. principal distinction is critical for revenue presentation. A principal reports gross revenue; an agent reports only the net commission. Although gross profit is identical in dollar terms, the gross margin differs dramatically. Similarly, in franchising and licensing, the franchisor must allocate transaction prices across distinct performance obligations — such as the license itself versus ongoing royalties — and recognize each component as those obligations are satisfied.
key_points: Revenue is recognized when earned under accrual accounting — not when cash is collected; credit sales create accounts receivable, prepayments create unearned revenue (liability).

Memorize the 5-step revenue recognition model: (1) Identify contract, (2) Identify performance obligations, (3) Determine transaction price, (4) Allocate price to obligations, (5) Recognize revenue when obligation is satisfied.
For long-term contracts, revenue is recognized based on progress toward completion using either input methods (% of costs incurred) or output methods (milestones, % of output delivered).

Agent vs. principal: An agent recognizes only net revenue (commission); a principal recognizes gross revenue. Gross profit in dollars is equal either way, but gross margin % is very different — this is a favorite exam trap.

A performance obligation is satisfied over time if: (a) customer receives ongoing benefit, (b) supplier creates/enhances an asset the customer controls during the process, or (c) asset has no alternative use and supplier has enforceable right to payment for work done to date.
Costs to obtain a long-term contract (e.g., sales commissions) must be capitalized and amortized over the contract period — they cannot be expensed immediately.

IFRS and U.S. GAAP are converged on the five-step model but differ on the definition of 'probable' collectability, meaning identical transactions can still be accounted for differently under each framework.

readings.title: Analyzing Balance Sheets Module 31.1: Intangible Assets And
readings.summary:
Intangible assets are nonmonetary, non-physical assets that are either identifiable (patents, trademarks, copyrights — separable or rights-based) or unidentifiable (goodwill — inseparable, potentially unlimited life). Under IFRS, purchased identifiable intangibles can be reported using the cost model or revaluation model (if an active market exists), while U.S. GAAP only permits the cost model. Internally created intangibles are generally expensed as incurred under both standards, with one key IFRS exception: development-stage costs can be capitalized when specific criteria are met (technical feasibility, available resources, existing market, and management intent). Research-stage costs are always expensed under IFRS, and all R&D is expensed under U.S. GAAP.

Finite-lived intangible assets are amortized over their useful lives and tested for impairment similarly to PP&E, with annual reviews of useful life and amortization method. Indefinite-lived intangibles (including goodwill) are not amortized but must be tested for impairment at least annually. Costs such as start-up, training, advertising, administrative overhead, relocation, and termination expenses are always expensed as incurred under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP.

Goodwill arises only from business acquisitions and equals the excess of purchase price over the fair value of identifiable net assets. It reflects unrecorded value such as brand reputation, customer loyalty, off-balance-sheet R&D, and expected synergies. Goodwill is an indefinite-lived asset — never amortized, but tested annually for impairment. A company may manipulate earnings by allocating more acquisition price to goodwill (not amortized) and less to depreciable identifiable assets, inflating future net income. Analysts should eliminate goodwill and impairment charges when comparing companies.

Financial instruments on the balance sheet are measured at historical cost, amortized cost, or fair value depending on the instrument type and management's intent. Trading securities and securities designated at fair value through profit or loss are marked to fair value with unrealized gains/losses in the income statement. Available-for-sale (IFRS: fair value through OCI) securities are at fair value with unrealized gains/losses in OCI (equity). Held-to-maturity securities are at amortized cost. Fair value measurements use a three-level hierarchy: Level 1 (quoted market prices) is most reliable, Level 2 (observable inputs) is intermediate, and Level 3 (unobservable/model-based inputs) is least reliable and most subject to manipulation.
key_points: IFRS allows cost or revaluation model for purchased intangibles; U.S. GAAP allows cost model only. Revaluation requires an active market.
Under IFRS, research costs are always expensed; development costs are capitalized if the project meets all feasibility/resource/market/intent criteria. Under U.S. GAAP, all R&D is expensed.
Goodwill = purchase price minus fair value of identifiable net assets; only created in acquisitions (never internally generated); never amortized but tested for impairment at least annually.
Finite-lived intangibles are amortized + impairment tested; indefinite-lived intangibles (including goodwill) skip amortization but require annual impairment testing.
Start-up costs, training, advertising, admin overhead, relocation, and termination costs are always expensed as incurred under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP.
For ratio analysis, analysts should remove goodwill from the balance sheet and exclude goodwill impairment charges from the income statement to improve comparability across companies.

Fair value hierarchy: Level 1 (quoted prices) > Level 2 (observable inputs) > Level 3 (unobservable/model inputs). Unrealized gains/losses on trading securities go to income statement; on available-for-sale/FVOCI securities go to OCI.

readings.title: Analyzing Statements Of Cash Flows I
readings.summary:
The cash flow statement bridges the income statement and balance sheet by reconciling beginning and ending cash balances through operating, investing, and financing activities. Unlike the income statement, which is accrual-based, the cash flow statement reflects actual cash inflows and outflows. Analysts use it to assess liquidity, solvency, and financial flexibility — including whether a firm generates enough cash to sustain operations, repay debts, and seize new opportunities.

Earnings quality is closely tied to the cash flow statement: high-quality earnings occur when operating cash flow (CFO) is close to or exceeds net income. If net income persistently exceeds CFO, it signals that accrual-based earnings are not being backed by real cash generation, which is a red flag. Operating activities generally relate to current assets and liabilities; investing activities to noncurrent assets; and financing activities to noncurrent liabilities and equity.

The direct method of presenting CFO shows actual cash receipts and payments line by line (e.g., cash collected from customers, cash paid to suppliers, cash paid for taxes). To convert an income statement item to its cash equivalent, you start with the revenue or expense, then adjust for changes in the related balance sheet accounts: subtract increases in assets or add decreases in assets; add increases in liabilities or subtract decreases in liabilities. For example, cash collected from customers = sales − increase in accounts receivable.

The indirect method starts with net income and works backward by adding back non-cash charges (e.g., depreciation, amortization) and adjusting for changes in working capital accounts. Both methods yield the same CFO figure; they only differ in presentation. CFI and CFF are reported identically regardless of which CFO method is used. Understanding the mechanical relationships between balance sheet changes and cash flows is essential both for exam calculations and for detecting potential earnings manipulation.
key_points: CFO = net income adjusted for non-cash items and working capital changes (indirect); or direct cash receipts minus payments (direct) — both yield the same CFO number.
High earnings quality: CFO ≥ net income. Persistently higher net income than CFO is a red flag indicating accruals are not backed by cash.

Direct method adjustment rule: increase in asset → subtract (use of cash); decrease in asset → add (source of cash); increase in liability → add (source of cash); decrease in liability → subtract (use of cash).
Cash collected from customers = Sales − Increase in A/R (or + Decrease in A/R). Cash paid to suppliers = COGS + Increase in Inventory − Increase in A/P.
Operating activities ↔ current assets & liabilities; Investing activities ↔ noncurrent assets; Financing activities ↔ noncurrent liabilities & equity.

Unearned revenue: cash is received (and shown in CFO) when the customer pays, but revenue is recognized on the income statement over the contract life — these timing differences are key for adjustments.
CFI and CFF presentation is identical under both direct and indirect methods — only CFO presentation differs between the two methods.

readings.title: Analyzing Statements Of Cash Flows II
readings.summary:
The cash flow statement is a critical tool for assessing a firm's liquidity, solvency, and financial flexibility. Analysts use it to determine whether operations generate sufficient cash to sustain the business, repay debt, meet unexpected obligations, and capitalize on new opportunities. A firm's life cycle stage shapes its cash flow pattern: early-stage firms often have negative operating cash flow financed externally, but long-term viability requires positive operating cash flow that exceeds capital expenditures and provides returns to investors.

Operating cash flow quality is a key analytical focus. Sustainable operating cash flow should come from earnings-related activities, not from one-time reductions in working capital like liquidating inventory or stretching payables. A stable relationship between net income and operating cash flow signals earnings quality; when earnings significantly exceed operating cash flow, it may indicate aggressive revenue recognition or deferred expense recognition.

Investing and financing activities also carry important signals. Rising capital expenditures indicate growth, while asset sales or capex cuts may signal cash stress and future replacement costs. The financing section reveals whether the firm is raising or retiring debt and equity, paying dividends, or repurchasing stock — all of which inform the analyst's view of capital allocation and financial strategy.

Common-size cash flow statements express each line item as a percentage of revenue (or as a share of total inflows/outflows). This format is especially useful for trend analysis and forecasting, since items tied to revenue can be projected once future revenue is estimated. Free cash flow measures — FCFF (available to all capital providers) and FCFE (available to common equity) — are central valuation inputs. FCFF adjusts CFO for after-tax interest and fixed capital investment; FCFE further adjusts for net borrowing. Cash flow ratios such as cash flow to debt and interest coverage complement these measures in evaluating financial health.
key_points: FCFF = CFO + [Interest Paid × (1 − tax rate)] − Fixed Capital Investment; it represents cash available to BOTH debt and equity holders.
FCFE = CFO − Fixed Capital Investment + Net Borrowing; it represents cash available to common shareholders only.
For IFRS firms: if interest paid is classified as financing (not operating), no CFO adjustment is needed for FCFF; if dividends paid are in CFO, add them back when computing FCFE.
Earnings quality check: operating cash flow should closely track net income over time; a large, persistent gap where net income >> CFO suggests aggressive or improper accounting.
Decreasing noncash working capital (liquidating inventory/receivables, stretching payables) can temporarily boost CFO but is not sustainable — flag this as a red flag in analysis.
Common-size cash flow statements use revenue as the base; this enables revenue-based forecasting and cross-period/cross-company comparisons of cash flow efficiency.
In the firm life cycle, early-stage firms have negative CFO funded externally; mature firms must generate CFO exceeding capex to be self-sustaining — a critical exam framework.

readings.title: Analysis Of Inventories Introduction
readings.summary:
This reading focuses on how inventory is measured and reported under IFRS and U.S. GAAP. Under IFRS, inventory is carried at the lower of cost or net realizable value (NRV), where NRV equals expected selling price minus estimated selling and completion costs. Under U.S. GAAP, companies using LIFO or the retail method use lower of cost or market, where 'market' is typically replacement cost but is constrained to fall within a range: no higher than NRV and no lower than NRV minus a normal profit margin. Companies using other methods under U.S. GAAP apply the same lower of cost or NRV standard as IFRS.

When inventory value falls below its carrying amount, a write-down is required and the loss flows through the income statement — either as a separate line item or embedded in COGS. Under IFRS, a subsequent recovery in value permits a write-up, but only up to the amount of the original write-down (i.e., carrying value cannot exceed original cost). Under U.S. GAAP, no write-up is ever allowed regardless of recovery; the written-down value becomes the new permanent cost basis, and gains are only realized when the inventory is eventually sold.

Inventory write-downs have broad ripple effects on financial ratios. In the period of the write-down, current assets and total assets decline (reducing the current ratio), equity falls (raising debt-to-equity), COGS rises (compressing gross, operating, and net margins), and both ROA and ROE decline — with net income falling proportionally more than assets or equity. In subsequent periods, the lower inventory carrying values reduce future COGS, improving profitability metrics and boosting reported ROA and ROE.

The reading also highlights the relationship between cost flow assumptions and write-down likelihood. Because LIFO ending inventory reflects older, typically lower costs (under inflation), LIFO firms are less likely to trigger write-downs compared to FIFO or weighted-average cost firms. Analysts must account for these differences when comparing firms across periods or across cost-flow methods. An exception to historical-cost measurement exists for commodity producers (agricultural, forest, mineral, precious metals) under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP, where inventory may be reported at NRV with unrealized gains and losses recognized in the income statement.
key_points: IFRS: inventory at lower of cost or NRV; write-downs allowed and write-ups permitted up to original cost. U.S. GAAP: NO write-ups ever — written-down value becomes permanent new cost basis.
Under U.S. GAAP (LIFO/retail method), 'market' = replacement cost, BUT constrained between NRV (ceiling) and NRV minus normal profit margin (floor). This range equals the normal profit margin.

Inventory write-down effects in the period: decreases current assets, current ratio, gross/operating/net margins, ROA, and ROE; increases inventory turnover, total asset turnover, debt-to-assets, and debt-to-equity ratios.
Post-write-down periods: lower inventory carrying values reduce future COGS, which INCREASES profitability and boosts ROA and ROE in subsequent periods — watch for this when comparing across time.
LIFO firms (under inflation) are LESS likely to recognize write-downs because their ending inventory already reflects older, lower costs, making book value less likely to exceed NRV or market.

Commodity exception (IFRS and U.S. GAAP): producers of agricultural products, forest products, mineral ores, and precious metals may report inventory ABOVE historical cost at NRV, with unrealized gains/losses in the income statement.
Quick ratio is UNAFFECTED by an inventory write-down because inventories are excluded from the quick ratio numerator — only the current ratio declines.

readings.title: Analysis Of Long-Term Assets Introduction
readings.summary:
Reading 35 focuses on the accounting and analysis of long-term assets, with particular emphasis on intangible assets. Intangible assets lack physical substance (e.g., patents, brand names, franchises) and are classified as either finite-lived (amortized over useful life) or indefinite-lived (not amortized, but tested for impairment at least annually). They are also categorized as identifiable (separable or arising from contractual/legal rights) or unidentifiable (e.g., goodwill, which cannot be separated from the business).

The accounting treatment of intangibles depends heavily on how they were obtained. Internally developed intangibles are generally expensed as incurred under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP. A key IFRS exception allows development costs (but not research costs) to be capitalized once specific criteria are met. Under U.S. GAAP, software developed for sale is expensed until technological feasibility is established, then capitalized; software for internal use is expensed until completion is probable.

Purchased intangibles are capitalized at cost (fair value at acquisition). When acquired in a business combination, the acquisition method requires allocating the purchase price to all identifiable assets and liabilities at fair value — including internally developed intangibles of the acquired firm that were previously expensed. Any remaining excess purchase price above the fair value of net identifiable assets is recorded as goodwill.

Impairment occurs when an asset's carrying value exceeds its recoverable amount. Under IFRS, the recoverable amount is the higher of fair value less costs to sell and value in use. Under U.S. GAAP, impairment is a two-step test: first check if carrying value exceeds undiscounted future cash flows; if so, write down to fair value. Impairment losses reduce net income and assets, and impairment of long-lived assets held for sale stops depreciation immediately. Reversals of impairment are allowed under IFRS but prohibited under U.S. GAAP.
key_points: IFRS vs. U.S. GAAP R&D: Under IFRS, research costs are expensed but development costs can be capitalized (if criteria met). Under U.S. GAAP, both R&D are expensed; software for sale is capitalized only after technological feasibility is established.

Only goodwill from a business combination is capitalized. Internally generated goodwill is always expensed. At acquisition, identifiable intangibles previously expensed by the target ARE capitalized — this reduces the amount recorded as goodwill.
Finite-lived intangibles are amortized; indefinite-lived intangibles are NOT amortized but must be tested for impairment at least annually.

Capitalizing vs. expensing: Capitalizing increases assets, equity, and operating cash flow in year 1, and increases net income in year 1 but decreases it in subsequent years due to depreciation/amortization.

Impairment testing differs: IFRS uses a one-step test (carrying value > recoverable amount = higher of fair value less costs to sell or value in use). U.S. GAAP uses a two-step test (first compare carrying value to undiscounted cash flows; then write down to fair value if impaired).
Impairment reversals: Allowed under IFRS (except goodwill); prohibited under U.S. GAAP.

A company that internally generates intangibles will show lower balance sheet assets and lower equity than a comparable company that purchases intangibles, distorting ratio comparisons (e.g., ROA, debt-to-equity).

readings.title: Topics In Long-Term Liabilities And Equity
readings.summary:
Reading 36 begins with lease accounting, explaining that a lease is a contract giving the lessee the right to use a specific asset while making periodic payments to the lessor. Leases offer advantages such as lower initial cash outflows, potentially cheaper financing (since the asset serves as collateral), and reduced obsolescence risk for the lessee. To qualify as a lease, a contract must reference a specific asset, transfer substantially all economic benefits during the term, and grant the lessee the right to direct the asset's use.

Leases are classified as either finance leases or operating leases. A lease is a finance lease if it meets any of five conditions: ownership transfers, a purchase option is expected to be exercised, the lease covers most of the asset's useful life, the PV of lease payments equals or exceeds the asset's fair value, or the asset is specialized with no alternative use. Under both IFRS and U.S. GAAP, the same lease classification applies to both lessor and lessee.

For lessee accounting, IFRS requires all non-short-term, non-low-value leases to record a right-of-use (ROU) asset and a lease liability on the balance sheet, both initially equal to the PV of future lease payments. For finance leases, the ROU asset is amortized straight-line while the lease liability is reduced by the principal portion of each payment — creating a temporary divergence between asset and liability book values. On the income statement, amortization expense and interest expense are reported separately; on the cash flow statement, principal repayment is a financing outflow, and interest is operating (U.S. GAAP) or operating/financing (IFRS).

For operating leases under U.S. GAAP, the ROU asset is amortized by the same amount as the lease liability decreases each period, keeping them equal throughout the lease term. The income statement reports a single combined lease expense (equal to the total lease payment), and the full payment appears as an operating cash outflow. This means operating leases have a neutral effect on operating income relative to a straight purchase, while finance leases front-load expenses due to the split between interest and amortization.

From the lessor's perspective, a finance lease removes the asset from the lessor's balance sheet and replaces it with a lease receivable, with income recognized as interest over time. For an operating lease, the lessor keeps the asset on its books, continues depreciating it, and recognizes lease income on a straight-line basis. Understanding these differences — and how they affect financial ratios such as leverage, coverage, and profitability — is central to the analyst adjustments tested on the CFA exam.
key_points: Finance lease triggers (any one suffices): ownership transfer, bargain purchase option expected to be exercised, lease covers most of useful life, PV of payments ≥ fair value, or asset is specialized with no alternative use for lessor.
Lessee balance sheet impact is the same for finance and operating leases: ROU asset and lease liability both recorded at PV of lease payments (IFRS always; U.S. GAAP for non-short-term leases).
Finance lease income statement: amortization expense (straight-line ROU asset) + interest expense (on declining liability) reported separately — this front-loads total expense vs. operating lease.
Operating lease (U.S. GAAP) income statement: single combined lease expense equal to the lease payment — ROU asset amortized by the same amount as the liability each period so they remain equal.
Cash flow classification: finance lease — principal repayment is CFFinancing, interest is CFOperating (U.S. GAAP) or CFO/CFF (IFRS); operating lease — entire payment is CFOperating under U.S. GAAP.
Lessor finance lease: derecognize asset, record lease receivable; income = interest on receivable. Lessor operating lease: keep asset on books, depreciate it, recognize rental income straight-line.

Analyst adjustment: converting an operating lease to a finance lease increases reported debt and assets, lowers operating income in early years, and shifts cash flow from CFO to CFF — worsening leverage and coverage ratios.

readings.title: Analysis Of Income Taxes Introduction
readings.summary:
Income tax accounting arises because financial reporting standards (IFRS and U.S. GAAP) differ from tax laws, causing a gap between what a company reports as income tax expense on its income statement and the taxes it actually owes (taxes payable) to tax authorities. Understanding the distinction between tax return terminology (taxable income, taxes payable, tax base) and financial reporting terminology (accounting profit, income tax expense, carrying value) is the essential foundation of this reading.

Differences between accounting profit and taxable income are classified as either permanent or temporary. Permanent differences never reverse and do not create deferred tax items. Temporary differences, by contrast, will reverse over time — the same total amount eventually passes through both the income statement and the tax return, just in different periods. These temporary differences give rise to deferred tax liabilities (DTLs) and deferred tax assets (DTAs) on the balance sheet.

A deferred tax liability is created when income tax expense exceeds taxes payable — meaning the company pays less tax now but will pay more later. The most common cause is using accelerated depreciation for tax purposes and straight-line depreciation for financial reporting. Because the timing difference will reverse and result in a future cash outflow, it meets the definition of a liability. Conversely, a deferred tax asset is created when taxes payable exceed income tax expense, meaning the company overpays tax now and will benefit (pay less) in the future. Common causes of DTAs include warranty expense accruals, unearned revenue, post-employment benefit obligations, and tax loss carryforwards.

If there is doubt about whether a DTA will actually be realized (i.e., whether the company will generate enough future taxable income to use it), a valuation allowance is established under U.S. GAAP to reduce the DTA to its expected realizable amount. Changes in the enacted tax rate also directly affect the carrying value of existing DTAs and DTLs, since they represent future tax effects that must be remeasured at the new rate.
key_points: Taxes payable (current tax expense) = tax owed per the tax return; Income tax expense = taxes payable ± changes in DTLs and DTAs on the income statement — do NOT confuse these two.
Permanent differences (e.g., tax-exempt interest, non-deductible fines) never reverse and create NO deferred tax assets or liabilities.
Temporary differences DO reverse over time and create DTLs (taxable temporary differences) or DTAs (deductible temporary differences).

DTL is created when income tax expense > taxes payable; most classic cause is accelerated depreciation on the tax return vs. straight-line on financial statements — firm pays less tax now, more later.

DTA is created when taxes payable > income tax expense; common causes are warranty expense, unearned revenue, post-employment benefits, and tax loss carryforwards — firm pays more tax now, saves later.
A valuation allowance (U.S. GAAP) reduces a DTA when it is more likely than not that the asset will not be fully realized; increasing the allowance reduces net income.
A change in the enacted tax rate requires remeasurement of all existing DTAs and DTLs at the new rate immediately, flowing the adjustment through income tax expense in the period of enactment.

readings.title: Financial Reporting Quality Module 38.1: Reporting Quality
readings.summary:
Financial reporting quality and the quality of reported results are distinct but related concepts. Financial reporting quality refers to how well financial statements adhere to GAAP and serve as decision-useful documents. High-quality financial reports must be both relevant (material and useful for decisions) and faithfully representative (complete, neutral, and free of errors). Importantly, GAAP compliance alone does not guarantee high reporting quality, since GAAP allows significant management discretion in methods, estimates, and treatments.

The quality of reported earnings is a separate dimension focused on sustainability and adequacy. Sustainable earnings are those likely to recur in future periods — earnings driven by operational efficiency or growing market share qualify, while one-time gains from asset sales or favorable exchange rate movements do not. High-quality earnings command higher firm valuations because they contribute more reliably to the present value of future cash flows. Earnings must also be adequate enough to sustain operations and provide investors with acceptable returns.

A spectrum of financial reporting quality ranges from best (GAAP-compliant, decision-useful, sustainable, and adequate earnings) to worst (fictitious or fraudulent numbers). Between these extremes lie reports that are GAAP-compliant but biased, earnings-managed, or non-compliant with real underlying data. It is entirely possible for a firm to have high reporting quality but low earnings quality — the statements can be technically accurate and GAAP-compliant yet still reflect unsustainable or inadequate earnings.

Conservative accounting reduces current-period reported earnings and balance sheet strength, while aggressive accounting inflates them. Neither extreme is inherently virtuous — both represent deviations from the ideal of neutral, unbiased reporting. Management often uses these levers to smooth earnings over time, adjusting accrued liabilities upward in good periods (deferring recognition, the 'cookie jar' strategy) and downward in weaker periods to meet expectations. Transparency in presentation — how much disclosure management provides — is another dimension of reporting quality beyond the numbers themselves.
key_points: Financial reporting quality (HOW results are reported) and earnings quality (WHAT is reported — sustainability and adequacy) are two separate dimensions; a firm can have high reporting quality but low earnings quality.
High-quality financial reports must satisfy two criteria: relevance (material and decision-useful) and faithful representation (complete, neutral, free of errors).
The six-level spectrum from best to worst: (1) GAAP-compliant + sustainable/adequate earnings → (6) fictitious/fraudulent; memorize that levels 3-4 are still GAAP-compliant but biased or managed.

Conservative accounting decreases current earnings/financial position (increases future earnings); aggressive accounting does the opposite — neither is automatically 'good' or 'bad'; both deviate from neutrality.
'Cookie jar' reserves: management uses conservative bias in high-earnings periods to defer income into accrued liabilities, then reverses those liabilities in low-earnings periods to smooth results.
Sustainable earnings sources (exam favorite): operational efficiency and market share gains = sustainable; one-time asset sales, favorable FX movements = NOT sustainable.

Balance sheet quality matters for earnings quality: understated liabilities or overstated assets reduce earnings quality and raise sustainability concerns even when the income statement looks acceptable.

readings.title: Financial Analysis Techniques
readings.summary:
Financial Analysis Techniques introduces the core analytical tools used to interpret financial statements. The primary tools are ratio analysis, common-size analysis, graphical analysis, and regression analysis. Each serves to transform raw financial data into formats that enable meaningful comparison across firms and time periods. Analysts must understand not just how to calculate each ratio, but what underlying firm characteristic it measures and whether a higher or lower value is preferable in context.

Ratio analysis expresses relationships between financial statement items and is used to project earnings and cash flows, assess management performance, evaluate flexibility, and benchmark against competitors. However, ratios have important limitations: they are meaningless in isolation, comparability is distorted by differing accounting treatments (especially across IFRS and US GAAP firms), multi-industry firms are hard to benchmark, and no single ratio tells the full story. Analysts must also be consistent in their own definitions, since ratio definitions vary across the analytical community.

Common-size analysis normalizes financial statements to make comparisons easier. A vertical common-size balance sheet expresses all items as a percentage of total assets; a vertical common-size income statement expresses all items as a percentage of revenue. This immediately reveals gross margin, operating margin, and net margin trends. A horizontal common-size statement uses a base-year value of 1.0, making multi-year growth trends readily visible. Both vertical and horizontal formats are commonly tested.

The DuPont decomposition of ROE is a central analytic technique. The basic DuPont formula breaks ROE into net profit margin × asset turnover × equity multiplier (leverage). The extended 5-factor DuPont further decomposes net profit margin into tax burden, interest burden, and EBIT margin, allowing analysts to isolate exactly which driver is responsible for changes in ROE. Understanding which component is improving or deteriorating is more valuable than observing ROE alone.

Credit analysis ratios assess a firm's ability to service debt and include interest coverage (EBIT/interest), fixed charge coverage, and debt-to-EBITDA. Liquidity ratios (current, quick, cash) measure short-term obligations. Activity ratios (receivables turnover, inventory turnover, payables turnover) measure operational efficiency and drive the cash conversion cycle. Profitability ratios (gross margin, operating margin, net margin, ROA, ROE) measure how effectively management generates returns. All ratio categories must be interpreted together and in the context of industry norms and business cycle position.
key_points: DuPont ROE decomposition: ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier; the 5-factor extension separates tax burden (NI/EBT), interest burden (EBT/EBIT), EBIT margin, asset turnover, and leverage — know all five components and what each signals.

Vertical common-size income statement expresses each line as % of revenue (reveals margins directly); vertical common-size balance sheet uses total assets as denominator; horizontal common-size uses a base year = 1.0 to show growth trends.

Ratio limitations to memorize: (1) useless in isolation, (2) accounting method differences impair cross-firm comparisons, (3) multi-segment firms are hard to benchmark, (4) no single ratio is conclusive, (5) ratio definitions are inconsistent across analysts.

Liquidity ratios — Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities; Quick Ratio = (Cash + Short-term Securities + Receivables) / Current Liabilities; Cash Ratio = (Cash + Short-term Securities) / Current Liabilities. Higher = more liquid.

Activity (efficiency) ratios drive the cash conversion cycle: DSO = 365/Receivables Turnover, DIO = 365/Inventory Turnover, DPO = 365/Payables Turnover; Cash Conversion Cycle = DSO + DIO − DPO. Lower CCC = faster cash generation.

Solvency/coverage ratios for credit analysis: Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest Expense; Fixed Charge Coverage = (EBIT + Lease Payments) / (Interest + Lease Payments); Debt-to-EBITDA measures leverage capacity. Higher coverage = safer.

Ratios must be evaluated in context of: prior period trends, company strategy, analyst expectations, stage in the business cycle, and industry peer comparisons — exam vignettes often require identifying which contextual factor explains a ratio change.

readings.title: Introduction To Financial Statement Modeling
readings.summary:
A sales-based pro forma model projects a company's future financial statements starting from estimated revenues. The eight-step process flows logically: estimate revenue growth first, then project COGS and SG&A as percentages of sales, estimate financing costs and taxes, model the balance sheet from income statement drivers (working capital accounts), project capital expenditures and net PP&E, and finally construct the cash flow statement from the completed income statement and balance sheet. Revenue estimates can be grounded in market growth and market share analysis, trend growth rates, or GDP-relative growth.

Analyst forecasts are vulnerable to five key behavioral biases. Overconfidence bias leads analysts to use confidence intervals that are too narrow; it is mitigated by soliciting peer critique and reviewing past forecast errors. Illusion of control bias causes analysts to over-engineer models with excessive variables or rely on cherry-picked 'expert' opinions; the remedy is to focus only on variables with proven explanatory power. Conservatism bias (anchoring) results in under-reaction to new information, especially negative news; simpler, more flexible models and regular forecast reviews help correct it.

Representativeness bias causes analysts to force new information into familiar classifications, neglecting the base rate (the outside view) in favor of firm-specific factors (the inside view). The best approach combines both perspectives. Confirmation bias leads analysts to seek out information that confirms existing views and dismiss contradictory evidence; reading opposing analyst reports and consulting colleagues with no prior position in the stock are the main antidotes. Analysts must also be vigilant about taking management commentary at face value.

Porter's Five Forces framework provides a structured way to assess how a company's competitive environment shapes its pricing power and cost pressures. Pricing power is higher when substitute threats are low, switching costs are high, industry rivalry is low, customer bargaining power is weak, and barriers to entry are high. Margins face more pressure when suppliers are concentrated and powerful, or when rivalry is intense due to commoditized products, high fixed costs, and slow industry growth.

Forecasting must also account for inflation and deflation in input costs and output prices. When input costs rise faster than output prices, margins compress; analysts must separately track volume growth versus price changes in both revenues and costs. Commodity-intensive industries require particular attention to input price trends, hedging strategies, and the ability to pass through cost increases to customers.
key_points: The 8-step pro forma model order: Revenue → COGS → SG&A → Financing costs → Taxes → Balance sheet (working capital) → CapEx/PP&E → Cash flow statement.
Overconfidence bias = intervals too narrow; fix by peer review, scenario analysis, and reviewing own past errors.
Illusion of control bias = overly complex models or seeking confirming 'experts'; fix by limiting variables to those with proven explanatory power.
Conservatism bias (anchoring) = slow to update forecasts on new info; fix with simpler models and periodic error reviews.
Representativeness bias = base-rate neglect (inside view only); fix by combining inside view (firm-specific) with outside view (industry base rate).
Confirmation bias = ignoring contradictory data; fix by reading opposing analysts and consulting unbiased colleagues; be skeptical of management's self-serving commentary.

Porter's Five Forces: pricing power is HIGH when substitutes are scarce, rivalry is low, customers are fragmented, entry barriers are high, and suppliers are weak — memorize which direction each force pushes margins.

readings.title: Market Organization And Structure
readings.summary:
The financial system serves three core functions: enabling entities to save, borrow, raise capital, manage risks, and trade assets; determining equilibrium interest rates that balance total savings supply with borrowing demand; and allocating capital to its most productive uses. These functions work best when markets are liquid, transaction costs are low, information is transparent, and regulation enforces contracts. Participants include individuals, firms, governments, and charities, all of whom interact through savings vehicles, debt issuance, equity markets, and derivative instruments.

Assets are broadly divided into financial assets (securities, derivatives, currencies) and real assets (real estate, equipment, commodities). Financial securities are further split into debt (promises to repay) and equity (ownership claims). Markets are classified by delivery timing (spot vs. forward/futures/options), by security maturity (money markets for ≤1 year vs. capital markets for longer-term), by issuance stage (primary vs. secondary), and by asset type (traditional debt/equity vs. alternative assets like hedge funds, real estate, and collectibles).

Major asset types include fixed-income securities (bills, notes, bonds, commercial paper, CDs, repos), equity securities (common and preferred shares), pooled vehicles (mutual funds, ETFs, hedge funds), currencies, derivative contracts (forwards, futures, options, swaps), commodities (energy, grains, metals, livestock), and real assets. Each has distinct characteristics regarding maturity, liquidity, risk, and how it is valued and traded in organized markets.

Financial intermediaries—brokers, dealers, exchanges, clearinghouses, arbitrageurs, securitizers, depository institutions, insurance companies, and investment banks—facilitate trading, provide liquidity, reduce transaction costs, and manage counterparty risk. Brokers act as agents matching buyers and sellers, while dealers commit their own capital by making markets. Clearinghouses act as the buyer to every seller and seller to every buyer, virtually eliminating counterparty default risk in exchange-traded markets.

Market structures vary by how orders are matched and executed. Quote-driven (dealer) markets rely on dealer bid-ask quotes; order-driven markets use limit order books or batch auctions to match buyers and sellers directly; brokered markets are used for unique assets requiring active search. Well-functioning markets are characterized by timely and accurate information, low transaction costs, liquidity, and fair operational efficiency, which together ensure that prices reflect fundamental values and capital flows to its highest-valued uses.
key_points: The three functions of the financial system are: (1) facilitate saving, borrowing, equity issuance, risk management, and asset exchange; (2) determine equilibrium interest rates; (3) allocate capital efficiently.

Asset classification hierarchy: Financial assets (securities, derivatives, currencies) vs. Real assets (real estate, equipment, commodities); within securities, debt vs. equity; within markets, primary vs. secondary and money markets (≤1 year) vs. capital markets.

Derivative contracts derive value from an underlying asset; forwards and futures obligate both parties, while options give the buyer the RIGHT but NOT the obligation to buy/sell at a predetermined price.
Brokers are agents who match buyers/sellers (earn commissions); dealers are principals who buy/sell from their own inventory (earn bid-ask spread) — know the distinction cold.
Clearinghouses eliminate counterparty risk by becoming the buyer to every seller and seller to every buyer; margin requirements and daily mark-to-market (variation margin) further protect the system.
Order types: market orders execute immediately at best available price; limit orders execute only at a specified price or better; stop orders become market orders once a trigger price is reached.

Alternative assets (hedge funds, real estate, commodities, collectibles) are typically illiquid, harder to value, require investor due diligence, and often trade at a discount relative to intrinsic value.

readings.title: Security Market Indexes Module 42.1: Index Weighting Methods
readings.summary:
A security market index represents the performance of an asset class, market, or market segment by tracking a portfolio of constituent securities. Index values are calculated from market prices at a point in time, and returns are expressed either as price returns (price changes only) or total returns (prices plus income such as dividends and interest). Multiple period returns are geometrically linked by compounding: RP = (1 + RS1)(1 + RS2) − 1.

Index construction requires several key decisions: defining the target market (broad or narrow), selecting constituent securities (all or a representative sample), choosing a weighting method, setting a rebalancing schedule, and determining when to re-examine the index composition. These choices significantly affect what the index measures and how it behaves over time.

The five main weighting methods are price weighting, equal weighting, market-cap weighting, float-adjusted market-cap weighting, and fundamental weighting. A price-weighted index is a simple arithmetic average of constituent prices — higher-priced stocks have more influence, and the divisor is adjusted for splits and composition changes. The DJIA and Nikkei are the primary examples. An equal-weighted index gives each constituent the same weight regardless of price or size, requiring frequent rebalancing that generates high transaction costs; the Value Line Composite is a key example.

Market capitalization-weighted indexes weight each stock by its market cap as a proportion of total index market cap, more accurately reflecting aggregate investor wealth. Float-adjusted market-cap indexes refine this by excluding shares held by controlling shareholders (and sometimes foreign-restricted shares), so weights better reflect shares actually available to investors. The main drawback of cap-weighted indexes is that overvalued stocks receive larger weights, introducing momentum bias. Fundamental weighting (by metrics like earnings, book value, or dividends) avoids this bias but is more complex and can tilt toward value stocks.
key_points: Price return uses only price changes; total return adds income (dividends/interest). Multi-period returns are geometrically linked: (1+R1)(1+R2)−1.

Price-weighted index = arithmetic average of prices. Higher-priced stocks have greater influence. Divisor is adjusted for splits/composition changes. Matching portfolio holds equal SHARES of each stock. Examples: DJIA (30 U.S. stocks), Nikkei (225 stocks).

Equal-weighted index = arithmetic average of returns. Matching portfolio holds equal DOLLAR amounts in each stock and must be rebalanced each period, creating high transaction costs. Overweights small-cap stocks relative to market value. Examples: Value Line Composite, FT Ordinary Share Index.

Market-cap weighted index weights each stock by (its market cap / total index market cap). Does NOT need adjustment for stock splits or stock dividends. Better reflects aggregate investor wealth but overweights overvalued stocks.

Float-adjusted market-cap index excludes shares held by controlling shareholders and sometimes foreign-restricted shares (free float). Weights reflect only shares actually available to investors — most major global indexes (MSCI, S&P 500) use this method.

Fundamental weighting uses metrics like earnings, book value, revenues, or dividends instead of price or market cap. Avoids the momentum/overvaluation bias of cap-weighting but tends to tilt toward value stocks and requires more complex rebalancing.

Key disadvantage of cap-weighted indexes: overvalued stocks get higher weights, so the index overexposures investors to expensive stocks — this is the primary criticism relative to fundamental weighting.

readings.title: Market Efficiency Module 43.1: Market Efficiency
readings.summary:
An informationally efficient capital market is one where security prices fully, quickly, and rationally reflect all available information. In such a market, prices are unbiased estimates of intrinsic value, meaning no investor can consistently earn positive risk-adjusted returns — commonly expressed as 'you can't beat the market.' The speed at which new information is incorporated into prices is a key measure of efficiency; in highly liquid markets like foreign currency markets, this lag can be as short as one minute. Importantly, only unexpected new information should move prices — anticipated announcements have no price impact because they are already priced in.

Market value is the current price of an asset, while intrinsic (fundamental) value is what a fully informed, rational investor would pay. In highly efficient markets, these two values converge. Active managers attempt to exploit gaps between market value and intrinsic value — buying undervalued and selling overvalued assets. However, intrinsic value is always an estimate and changes continuously as new information arrives, making it impossible to know with certainty.

Several factors determine a market's degree of efficiency: the number of active participants (more participants = more efficient), the availability and fairness of information (e.g., SEC Regulation FD mandates equal disclosure), impediments to trading such as high transaction costs or restrictions on short selling, and arbitrage constraints. Markets exist on a spectrum — neither perfectly efficient nor completely inefficient — and efficiency varies by country, asset class, and time period.

Eugene Fama formalized market efficiency into three forms. Weak-form efficiency holds that all past price and volume data is already reflected in prices, making technical analysis ineffective. Semi-strong-form efficiency states that all publicly available information — including financial statements, news, and economic data — is already priced in, making fundamental analysis unable to generate excess returns. Strong-form efficiency claims prices reflect all information, including private (insider) information, meaning no one can consistently earn abnormal returns. Evidence generally supports weak and semi-strong forms, but rejects strong-form efficiency given the existence of insider trading laws and empirical anomalies.
key_points: Three forms of EMH: Weak (past market data), Semi-strong (all public info), Strong (all public + private info) — each subsumes the previous form.
Weak form → technical analysis cannot generate excess returns; Semi-strong → fundamental analysis cannot; Strong → even insider information cannot.
Only UNEXPECTED new information moves prices; anticipated announcements are already priced in and have no effect.
Market value = current price; Intrinsic value = rational, fully-informed estimate of worth. Active managers trade on gaps between these two.
Factors increasing market efficiency: more participants, greater information availability, Reg FD (fair disclosure), low transaction costs, and ability to short sell.
Arbitrage and short selling are efficiency-enhancing mechanisms; restrictions on either allow mispricings to persist.
Evidence supports weak and semi-strong forms of EMH, but markets are NOT strong-form efficient — insider trading can and does generate abnormal returns, which is why it is prohibited.

readings.title: Overview Of Equity Securities
readings.summary:
Equity securities represent ownership interests in firms, with common shares being the most prevalent form. Common shareholders hold residual claims on assets after debt and preferred stockholders, have voting rights on board elections and major corporate decisions, and may receive dividends at the firm's discretion. Voting can occur under statutory voting (one vote per share per director seat) or cumulative voting (total votes pooled across all seats), with cumulative voting providing minority shareholders greater board representation.

Preferred shares (preference shares) are hybrid securities combining features of both debt and equity. They typically pay fixed dividends that are not contractually obligated, lack voting rights, and take priority over common shares in dividends and liquidation. Key variants include: cumulative preferred (unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before common dividends), non-cumulative preferred (missed dividends do not accumulate), participating preferred (receive extra dividends if profits exceed a threshold), non-participating preferred (capped at par value in liquidation), callable preferred (firm can repurchase at a set price), putable preferred (investor can sell back at a set price), and convertible preferred (can be exchanged for common shares at a preset ratio).

Private equity differs from public equity in several important ways: it is less liquid, priced through negotiation rather than markets, subject to lower disclosure and reporting requirements, potentially has weaker corporate governance, but can offer higher returns and allows management to focus on long-term goals. The three main private equity structures are venture capital (early-stage firm funding, illiquid, 3–10 year horizon), leveraged buyouts (LBOs, where a firm is acquired using significant debt; a management-led LBO is called an MBO), and private investments in public equity (PIPEs, where a public firm sells equity privately at a discount for quick capital).

Depository receipts (DRs) allow investors to hold foreign equities indirectly. American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) are U.S. dollar-denominated and trade on U.S. exchanges, while Global Depositary Receipts (GDRs) trade on exchanges outside the issuer's home country. Sponsored DRs involve cooperation from the foreign firm and carry full shareholder rights; unsponsored DRs are created without firm involvement and may have limited voting rights. Basket of Listed Depositary Receipts (BLDRs) are ETF-like instruments tracking an index of DRs.
key_points: Cumulative voting: total votes = shares × directors to be elected; allows minority shareholders to concentrate votes on one candidate and gain board representation — statutory voting does not allow this.

Preferred share dividend priority rule: cumulative preferred requires ALL missed dividends (arrears) to be paid before common shareholders receive any dividend; non-cumulative only requires the current period's dividend to be paid first.

Convertible preferred shares offer a floor (fixed preferred dividend) plus upside (conversion to common stock) — commonly used by venture capital and private equity investors to compensate for higher risk.

Private equity vs. public equity: private equity = less liquidity, negotiated pricing, lower disclosure, potentially weaker governance, but greater long-term focus and potentially higher returns upon IPO exit.
LBO = acquisition of a firm using primarily debt financing; MBO = LBO where the buyers are the firm's own management team.
PIPE (Private Investment in Public Equity): a public firm sells shares privately at a discount to market price to raise capital quickly — used by firms in distress, with growth needs, or high debt.
Sponsored ADRs give foreign shareholders full voting and dividend rights; unsponsored ADRs are bank-created without the foreign firm's direct involvement and may carry limited rights.

readings.title: Company Analysis: Past And Present
readings.summary:
A company research report is a structured document that includes an analyst's valuation, investment recommendation, and the supporting financial analysis. Initial reports (initiating coverage) are thorough and cover front matter, business model, industry overview, competitive positioning, financial analysis, valuation, ESG factors, and key risks. Subsequent reports are more focused, updating the recommendation, analyzing new information, and noting changes in valuation or risks since the prior report.

A company's business model describes how it creates and captures value, covering products/services, customers, sales channels, pricing, and supplier relationships. Analysts draw on four information types: direct company disclosures (filings, IR), public third-party sources (news, government data), proprietary third-party sources (Bloomberg, paid reports), and proprietary primary research (surveys, studies). Understanding the business model is foundational before any financial forecasting or valuation work.

Revenue analysis can be approached bottom-up (breaking revenue into price × volume, segments, or geography) or top-down (using macro variables like GDP growth or market share). Pricing power—the ability to raise prices without losing sales—is central to revenue analysis. Pricing power is high in markets with product differentiation, high switching costs, few substitutes, and high barriers to entry (monopoly/oligopoly), and low in highly competitive, commoditized markets where firms are price takers and economic profit trends toward zero.

Operating profitability analysis focuses on cost structure and working capital efficiency. Analysts examine gross margin, operating margin, and EBITDA margin to assess how well a company converts revenue into profit and whether margins are stable, expanding, or compressing over time. Working capital metrics such as days sales outstanding (DSO), days inventory outstanding (DIO), and days payable outstanding (DPO) reveal how efficiently a company manages its short-term assets and liabilities, with the cash conversion cycle (DSO + DIO − DPO) summarizing the net days of financing needed for operations.
key_points: Initial research reports cover 8 key sections: front matter, recommendation rationale, company description, industry/competitive overview, financial model, valuation, ESG factors, and key risks — subsequent reports focus on updates and variance analysis only.

Four sources of business model information: (1) direct company disclosures, (2) public third-party (news, gov), (3) proprietary third-party (Bloomberg), (4) proprietary primary research — Bloomberg is proprietary third-party, NOT public.

Pricing power is HIGH in markets with product differentiation, high switching costs, high barriers to entry, and few substitutes; pricing power is LOW (price takers) in commoditized, highly competitive markets where long-run economic profit → 0.
Exception to zero-profit competitive markets: a low-cost producer can earn returns above cost of capital, but only if the cost advantage is sustained permanently.
Revenue analysis: bottom-up = price × volume, segments, geography; top-down = macroeconomic drivers like GDP growth or market share. Analysts typically use both approaches together.
Rising profit margins over time (prices rising faster than costs) signal pricing power — the company is transferring cost increases to customers without losing sales volume.
Cash conversion cycle = DSO + DIO − DPO; a shorter cycle means less working capital needed, a longer cycle signals more financing required for operations — lower is generally better.

readings.title: Industry And Competitive Analysis
readings.summary:
Industry and competitive analysis is a macro-level framework used to assess what drives industry size, profitability, and market share, and to determine a company's competitive position within its industry. Different industries have structurally different long-run profitability levels, and competition tends to drive company profitability toward an industry base rate over time. While industry factors set a ceiling on profitability, company-specific factors—such as business model and competitive strategy—explain why individual firms outperform or underperform their peers. Analysts use industry analysis to improve financial forecasts, identify overlooked investment opportunities, and construct portfolios with targeted industry-level risk exposure.

The five steps of industry and competitive analysis are: (1) define the industry, (2) survey its size, growth, profitability, and market share trends, (3) analyze industry structure using frameworks like Porter's Five Forces, (4) examine external influences using PESTLE (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental), and (5) evaluate each company's competitive strategy and advantages within the industry context.

Companies are classified into industries using commercial systems such as GICS (S&P/MSCI), ICB (FTSE Russell), and TRBC (Refinitiv). All three systems share 11 common top-level sectors including Energy, Financials, Information Technology, Health Care, and Consumer Discretionary. Classification follows a hierarchy of 4–5 tiers and assigns firms based on their principal business activity, with revenue thresholds (>60%, then >50%) used to determine classification for multi-segment companies.

Classification systems have notable limitations. Industry groupings can be too broad or too narrow, multi-business companies are assigned to only one industry (creating comparability issues—e.g., Amazon classified as consumer retail despite IT profits driving results), geographic relevance varies by industry, and periodic updates disrupt historical data continuity. Analysts must be aware of these limitations and apply judgment when necessary.

Peer group analysis—a more targeted alternative—involves constructing a custom set of comparable companies for valuation and benchmarking purposes. Analysts typically start with commercial classifications and then refine the group based on business model similarity, size, geography, and financial characteristics. A well-constructed peer group is essential for meaningful relative valuation.
key_points: Know the 5 steps of industry analysis: Define → Survey → Structure (Porter's Five Forces) → External (PESTLE) → Competitive Strategy.
The three major commercial classification systems are GICS (S&P/MSCI), ICB (FTSE Russell), and TRBC (Refinitiv); all share 11 common top-level sectors.
Classification hierarchy differs by system — GICS: sector > industry group > industry > subindustry; ICB: industry > supersector > sector > subsector.
Multi-segment company classification rule: >60% revenue in one segment → classify there; if not met, use >50% of revenue/profits/assets; otherwise use judgment or 'conglomerate'.
Key limitation: a company is assigned to only ONE industry group, causing misclassification for diversified firms (e.g., Amazon labeled consumer discretionary despite cloud/IT dominance).
Competition drives company profitability toward the industry base rate over time — industry structure sets the long-run profitability ceiling, not company-specific factors alone.
PESTLE (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental) is the framework for analyzing external influences on an industry.

readings.title: Company Analysis: Forecasting
readings.summary:
Financial statement forecasts serve two primary purposes: valuation and investment recommendations. External forecasts focus on key metrics like revenue and EPS, while internal forecasts can be far more detailed. Analysts work with four types of forecast objects: (1) line items with clear drivers (e.g., number of stores × sales per store), which offer explanatory value but can be complex; (2) items without clear drivers, forecasted directly using management estimates or prior-year adjustments; (3) summary measures like EPS or free cash flow, which speed up the process but reduce transparency; and (4) ad hoc items such as contingent liabilities or one-time gains not yet reflected in financials. A key principle is to keep models as simple as necessary — complexity does not guarantee accuracy.

Four forecasting approaches can be used individually or in combination. The historical results approach uses past performance as a baseline and works best for mature, non-cyclical companies, but is unreliable for cyclical industries or firms undergoing strategic transitions. The historical base rate convergence approach assumes a metric (like growth rate) will revert to an industry average over time; it suits established industries with many publicly traded peers but is inappropriate for cyclical industries or dominant firms that effectively define the industry average themselves.

Management guidance is a third approach, where public company managers disclose revenue and earnings targets, often as ranges rather than point estimates. Because management has access to non-public internal and industry data, analysts pay close attention — but must scrutinize whether management's underlying assumptions (GDP growth, pricing, cost trends) are realistic. Managers are also known to shade guidance conservatively (lower revenue, higher expense ranges) to appear to beat expectations. Guidance is most useful when management has a proven track record of accuracy, verified through budget-versus-actual variance analysis. The fourth approach — analyst discretionary forecasts — is a catch-all using surveys, models, or probability distributions, most appropriate for cyclical companies, firms with no peers, companies offering no guidance, or those in major operational transitions.

Revenue forecasting uses two directional frameworks. Top-down analysis starts with macroeconomic conditions (GDP growth, industry growth) and narrows down to the company's expected market share and revenue. Bottom-up analysis aggregates company-level drivers such as unit volumes, pricing, and product mix, then sums them to derive total revenue. Analysts often use both approaches as a cross-check. The appropriate forecast horizon depends on the investor's time horizon, industry cyclicality (cyclical industries require at least a full business cycle), and company-specific factors such as strategic transitions whose benefits take time to materialize.
key_points: Four forecast object types: (1) line items with clear drivers, (2) items without clear drivers, (3) summary measures (EPS, FCF), (4) ad hoc items (contingent liabilities, one-time events) — know when each is appropriate.
Historical results approach: best for mature, non-cyclical companies; inappropriate for cyclical industries (use multi-year/full-cycle horizon) or firms undergoing strategic change.
Historical base rate convergence: assumes metric reverts to industry average; inappropriate for cyclical industries, new/rapidly changing industries, or dominant companies (they ARE the base rate).

Management guidance: presented as ranges, not point estimates; analysts must verify assumptions (GDP, pricing, costs) are realistic; managers systematically bias guidance conservatively (low revenue, high expense) to beat expectations.

Analyst discretionary forecasts are the catch-all approach, using surveys/models/probability distributions; best for cyclical companies, no-peer firms, no-guidance firms, or firms in major transitions.
Top-down revenue forecasting: macro → industry → company market share; bottom-up: aggregate unit volumes × prices by segment; use both as a cross-check for robustness.
Forecast horizon rule: for cyclical industries, the horizon must cover at least the midpoint of a full business cycle; for strategic changes, long enough to observe measurable benefits.

readings.title: Equity Valuation: Concepts And Basic Tools
readings.summary:
Equity valuation involves estimating a stock's intrinsic value and comparing it to the current market price to determine if the stock is overvalued, undervalued, or fairly valued. Analysts assume that market prices can deviate from intrinsic values, and those who can identify such deviations may earn abnormal profits. However, acting on a perceived mispricing requires confidence in the model used, the inputs applied, the size of the discrepancy, and the belief that the price will converge toward intrinsic value within the investor's time horizon.

There are three major categories of equity valuation models. Discounted cash flow (DCF) models estimate value as the present value of future cash flows to shareholders, including dividend discount models (DDM) and free cash flow to equity (FCFE) models. Multiplier models (market multiples) use ratios like P/E, P/S, P/B, P/CF, or EV/EBITDA to assess relative value. Asset-based models estimate equity value as total assets minus liabilities and preferred stock, adjusting book values to fair values.

Corporate actions such as dividends, stock splits, and share repurchases are important to understand for valuation purposes. Cash dividends (regular, special/extra) reduce equity and distribute cash to shareholders. Stock dividends and stock splits increase share count while reducing price proportionally — shareholder wealth is unchanged. Reverse splits do the opposite. Share repurchases are economically equivalent to cash dividends of equal size and may be preferred in tax regimes where capital gains are taxed lower than dividend income.

The dividend payment chronology defines key dates: the declaration date (board approves dividend), ex-dividend date (first day buyer does not receive dividend, typically 1-2 days before record date), holder-of-record date (shareholders on this date receive dividend), and payment date (dividend is actually paid). The ex-dividend date is the most tested — stock prices typically fall by approximately the dividend amount on this date.
key_points: To act on a mispricing, an investor needs: (1) large enough price-to-value gap, (2) confidence in the model, (3) confidence in inputs, (4) reason to believe the market is wrong, and (5) belief the price will converge within the investment horizon.
Three valuation model categories: DCF models (DDM, FCFE), Multiplier/Market Multiple models (P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA), and Asset-Based models (assets minus liabilities/preferred stock at fair value).
Stock dividends and stock splits do NOT change shareholder wealth — more shares, proportionally lower price. Reverse splits also leave wealth unchanged — fewer shares, higher price.
Share repurchases are equivalent in economic effect to cash dividends of the same size. They may be preferred when capital gains tax rates are lower than dividend tax rates.

Ex-dividend date is 1-2 business days BEFORE the holder-of-record date. A buyer on or after the ex-date does NOT receive the dividend. Stock price typically drops by approximately the dividend amount on the ex-date.
Enterprise value = market value of all outstanding securities MINUS cash and short-term investments. EV/EBITDA and EV/Revenue are the two enterprise value multiples used in valuation.
Special (extra/irregular) dividends are one-time payments common in cyclical industries — used to distribute profits in good times while preserving flexibility to conserve cash in downturns.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Instrument Features
readings.summary:
Fixed-income instruments are debt agreements where an issuer borrows capital (principal/par/face value) from investors and promises periodic interest payments (coupons) plus repayment of principal. The two main forms are loans (private, nontradable) and bonds (standardized, tradable securities). Issuers range from sovereign governments and corporations to local governments, supranational entities, quasi-government entities, and special purpose entities that issue asset-backed securities (ABS).

Key bond features include maturity (bonds with original maturities ≤1 year are money market securities; >1 year are capital market securities; no maturity date = perpetual bonds), coupon structure (fixed-rate, floating-rate notes tied to a market reference rate plus a spread, or zero-coupon/pure discount bonds), and seniority (senior debt ranks above subordinated/junior debt in bankruptcy). Floating-rate notes (FRNs) pay a variable market reference rate plus a fixed margin expressed in basis points. Zero-coupon bonds are issued at a discount and pay par at maturity with no interim cash flows.

Bond prices and yields are inversely related: when prices fall, yields rise, and vice versa. A yield curve plots yields against maturities for a given issuer. A normal (upward-sloping) yield curve reflects investor demand for higher returns at longer maturities due to greater uncertainty. An inverted (downward-sloping) yield curve is less common. Government bond yields serve as benchmarks, and the extra yield offered by riskier issuers is called the credit spread.

The bond indenture is the legal contract between issuer and bondholders, specifying repayment sources and borrower obligations. Secured bonds have a lien on specific collateral, while unsecured bonds rely solely on operating cash flows. Bond covenants are either affirmative (actions the issuer must take, such as making timely payments and maintaining insurance) or negative (restrictions on the issuer, such as limits on additional debt issuance or asset sales). Negative covenants are designed to protect bondholders from actions that could impair repayment.
key_points: Inverse price/yield relationship: fixed-coupon bond prices and yields always move in opposite directions — if yield rises, price falls, and vice versa.
Bond maturity classification: original maturity ≤1 year = money market security; >1 year = capital market security; no maturity = perpetual bond.
Coupon types: fixed-rate (set % of par), floating-rate note (FRN = MRR + fixed spread in bps), zero-coupon (issued at discount, pays par at maturity, no interim coupons).
Seniority hierarchy: senior secured > senior unsecured > subordinated (junior) debt > equity — higher seniority means lower credit risk.
Yield curve shapes: normal = upward-sloping (most common, reflects risk premium for longer maturities); inverted = downward-sloping (less common, often signals recession expectations).

Bond covenants: affirmative covenants require the issuer to DO something (e.g., pay coupons, maintain collateral); negative covenants restrict the issuer from doing something (e.g., taking on excess debt, selling pledged assets).

Credit spread = yield on risky bond minus yield on same-maturity government benchmark bond; government bonds are the lowest credit risk benchmark in a market due to tax-raising and currency-issuing powers.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Cash Flows And Types
readings.summary: Fixed-income instruments vary primarily in how they return principal to investors. The most common structure is the bullet bond, where the full par value is repaid at maturity and periodic coupon payments are purely interest. In contrast, amortizing loans (fully or partially) blend principal repayment into each periodic payment, reducing the outstanding balance over time. A partially amortizing bond ends with a balloon payment of remaining principal. These structural differences affect both the cash flow profile and the risk characteristics of the instrument.
key_points: Bullet bond: principal repaid at maturity, coupons are interest-only. Fully amortizing: equal payments blend interest + principal (use N, I/Y, PV, FV=0, CPT PMT). Partially amortizing: set FV = balloon amount in calculator.
Sinking fund provisions reduce credit risk by retiring principal periodically, but create reinvestment risk for bondholders when rates fall (early cash flows reinvested at lower yields).
Floating-rate notes (FRNs) pay MRR + credit spread (in basis points). Quarterly coupon = (MRR + spread) / 4. The spread compensates for issuer credit risk above the reference rate.
Step-up coupon bonds have predetermined coupon increases over time; credit-linked notes have coupons tied to issuer credit rating changes (coupon rises if credit deteriorates).
Payment-in-kind (PIK) bonds pay interest by increasing principal outstanding rather than cash — signal of high leverage and cash flow risk; carry higher yields.

Inflation-linked bonds: Capital-indexed (TIPS) keep coupon rate fixed but adjust principal for CPI — most common and most tested. Interest-indexed bonds adjust the coupon rate but leave principal unchanged. TIPS floor: investors receive max of inflation-adjusted or original par at maturity.
Waterfall structures in ABS/MBS distribute principal sequentially — senior tranches receive all principal repayments before junior tranches; interest is paid to all tranches regardless of seniority.

readings.title: Fixed-Income Issuance And Trading
readings.summary:
Global bond markets are segmented primarily by issuer type, credit quality, and maturity. Major issuer categories include governments (sovereign and non-sovereign), corporates, and special purpose entities issuing asset-backed securities. Credit quality divides bonds into investment-grade (AAA/Aaa down to BBB-/Baa3) and high-yield or 'junk' bonds (BB+/Ba1 and below).
