These bounds reflect the time value of money: because European options can only be exercised at expiration, the exercise price is discounted at the risk-free rate. Violating these bounds creates riskless profit opportunities.

Put-call parity is a foundational relationship linking European call and put prices on the same underlying, strike, and expiration: c₀ + X·(1+Rf)^-T = p₀ + S₀. This fiduciary call (long call plus risk-free bond) must equal the value of a protective put (long put plus long stock) to prevent arbitrage. Put-call parity allows synthetic replication of any component from the others, and violations imply riskless arbitrage by buying the cheaper side and selling the more expensive side.
key_points: Call moneyness: ITM if S > X, OTM if S < X; Put moneyness: ITM if X > S, OTM if X > S is false (X < S). Memorize the sign: call profits when S rises, put profits when S falls.
Exercise value (intrinsic value) = Max(0, S − X) for calls; Max(0, X − S) for puts. Option premium = exercise value + time value. Time value = 0 at expiration.
Upper bounds: European call ≤ S₀; European put ≤ X·(1+Rf)^-T (PV of strike, not strike itself, because early exercise is not allowed).
Lower bounds: European call ≥ Max[0, S₀ − X·(1+Rf)^-T]; European put ≥ Max[0, X·(1+Rf)^-T − S₀]. If the market price is below the lower bound, an arbitrage exists.
Put-call parity: c₀ + X·(1+Rf)^-T = p₀ + S₀. Left side = fiduciary call; right side = protective put. This equation only holds for European options on the same underlying, strike, and expiration.

Options differ from forwards: options have positive initial value (premium paid), asymmetric payoffs (buyer's loss capped at premium), and require replication-based pricing rather than simple cost-of-carry no-arbitrage.
Any portfolio with no possibility of a negative payoff must have a non-negative value today — this is the core no-arbitrage principle used to derive all option bounds and put-call parity.

readings.title: Option Replication Using Put–Call Parity
readings.summary:
Put-call parity is derived by comparing two portfolios with identical payoffs at expiration: a fiduciary call (long call + risk-free zero-coupon bond paying X at expiration) and a protective put (long stock + long put with strike X). Regardless of whether the stock price ends above or below X, both portfolios pay either S or X, making their payoffs identical in all scenarios. Because no-arbitrage pricing requires identical payoffs to command identical prices, the relationship c + X(1+Rf)^-T = S + p must hold for European options with the same strike and expiration.

From this core equation, any one of the four securities can be synthetically replicated by combining the other three. A long stock equals long call + short put + long bond; a long put equals long call + short stock + long bond; a long call equals long stock + long put + short bond; and a risk-free bond equals long stock + long put + short call. The sign of each term directly indicates whether you go long (+) or short (-) in that instrument.

Put-call-forward parity extends this concept by replacing the underlying asset with a synthetic asset created via a forward contract. Because entering a forward costs nothing upfront, a long forward plus a bond paying the forward price F0(T) at time T replicates owning the asset today at cost F0(T)(1+Rf)^-T. Substituting this for S in the standard parity equation gives: c0 + X(1+Rf)^-T = F0(T)(1+Rf)^-T + p0, or equivalently p0 - c0 = [X - F0(T)](1+Rf)^-T.

Put-call parity also has a powerful application in corporate finance. Equity holders' claim on a firm can be viewed as a call option on firm value V with strike equal to the face value of debt D — they receive Max(0, V-D). Debt holders' claim is equivalent to a long risk-free bond paying D minus a short put on firm value with strike D — they receive Min(V, D). This framework connects derivatives pricing directly to capital structure theory.
key_points: Memorize the core equation: c + X(1+Rf)^-T = S + p. Fiduciary call = protective put; both pay Max(S, X) at expiration.
To create any synthetic security, rearrange parity so the target is alone on the left; a + sign means long, a - sign means short in that instrument.
Synthetic stock: long call + short put + long bond. Synthetic put: long call + short stock + long bond. Synthetic call: long stock + long put + short bond.
Put-call-forward parity substitutes F0(T)(1+Rf)^-T for S: c0 + X(1+Rf)^-T = F0(T)(1+Rf)^-T + p0. The underlying asset itself is NOT required.
All four parity relationships require European-style options with the same exercise price X and the same expiration date T.
Corporate finance application: equity = call on firm value (strike = debt face value D); debt = long risk-free bond + short put on firm value (strike = D).
Arbitrage enforcement: if parity is violated, the cheaper portfolio is bought and the more expensive one is sold short to lock in a riskless profit, driving prices back to parity.

readings.title: Valuing A Derivative Using A One-Period Binomial Model
readings.summary:
The one-period binomial model prices options by assuming the underlying asset can move to only one of two possible values over a single period. To build the model, you need the current price of the underlying, the option's exercise price, the up-move and down-move return factors, and the risk-free rate. Crucially, the actual probabilities of an up- or down-move are not needed for the no-arbitrage approach.

The no-arbitrage method works by constructing a hedge portfolio — buying h shares of the underlying and writing one call option — such that the portfolio's value is identical regardless of which price move occurs. This hedge ratio h equals (c_u - c_d) / (S_u - S_d), the difference in option payoffs divided by the difference in stock prices. Because the hedged portfolio is risk-free, its present value equals the certain future payoff discounted at the risk-free rate, which then allows you to solve for the option's current price.

An alternative but equivalent approach uses risk-neutral probabilities. These pseudo-probabilities are derived mathematically from the up/down move factors and the risk-free rate — not from real-world expectations. The option value is then computed as the risk-neutral probability-weighted average of the up and down payoffs, discounted at the risk-free rate. This method works for both calls and puts; only the payoff calculations differ.

The key insight is that because both methods are grounded in a no-arbitrage relationship, the actual investor risk preferences are irrelevant. The risk-neutral probabilities are not real probabilities — they are a mathematical construct that allows discounting at the risk-free rate. In practice, multi-period binomial trees extend this concept, but the one-period model captures the essential logic.
key_points: Actual probabilities of up/down moves are NOT required for binomial option pricing — the no-arbitrage hedge eliminates the need for them.
Hedge ratio h = (c_u - c_d) / (S_u - S_d): the number of shares to buy per call written to create a risk-free portfolio.
No-arbitrage method: set up a hedge portfolio so V_u = V_d, discount the certain payoff at the risk-free rate, then solve c_0 = hS_0 - V_0.
Risk-neutral probabilities are pseudo-probabilities derived from move factors and the risk-free rate — they are NOT actual market probabilities.
Risk-neutral valuation steps: (1) compute option payoffs at expiration, (2) take risk-neutral probability-weighted average, (3) discount at the risk-free rate.
Both the no-arbitrage and risk-neutral probability approaches yield the same option value — they are mathematically equivalent.
The framework applies to both calls and puts; only the expiration payoff formula changes: call payoff = Max(0, S - X), put payoff = Max(0, X - S).

readings.title: Alternative Investment Performance And Returns
readings.summary:
Alternative investments carry additional risks beyond those of traditional long-only strategies, stemming from cash flow timing, leverage use, illiquid asset valuation, and complex fee structures. Evaluating their returns without accounting for these risks is considered naive and potentially misleading. Investors must understand that the unique characteristics of alternative funds fundamentally change how performance should be measured and interpreted.

The life cycle of an alternative investment fund typically follows a J-curve pattern across three phases: the capital commitment phase (negative returns as capital is called and deployed), the capital deployment phase (returns remain negative or low as investments mature), and the capital distribution phase (returns turn positive and peak). Because managers control the timing of cash flows, IRR (money-weighted return) is the most appropriate performance measure over a fund's life. The multiple of invested capital (money multiple) is simpler but ignores timing and is considered a naive metric.

Leverage amplifies both gains and losses. The leveraged return formula adjusts the base portfolio return by adding the spread between the investment return and borrowing cost, scaled by the leverage ratio. Risks include margin calls forcing liquidations at unfavorable prices, and lenders restricting access to additional credit. Valuation of illiquid assets uses a three-level fair value hierarchy: Level 1 (quoted market prices), Level 2 (observable inputs/models), and Level 3 (unobservable inputs like private equity or real estate). Level 3 valuations may be stale, making returns appear smoother, less risky, and less correlated with traditional assets than they truly are.

Fee structures in alternative investments are complex and include management fees and performance (incentive) fees. Key provisions include high-water marks (performance fees only charged on net new profits above the prior peak NAV), hard hurdle rates (fees only on returns above the hurdle), soft hurdle rates (fees on all returns once hurdle is exceeded), and clawback provisions (returning previously paid fees if later losses occur). Waterfall structures determine how distributions are split between the GP and LPs, either on a deal-by-deal basis or across the whole fund. Lockup periods, notice periods, redemption fees, and gates restrict investor redemptions to protect fund operations.
key_points: J-curve effect: Alternative fund returns are negative in early phases (capital commitment and deployment) and turn positive in the capital distribution phase — know all three phase names and their return characteristics.
IRR (money-weighted return) is the correct performance measure for alternative investments because managers control the timing of cash flows; the money multiple is simpler but ignores timing.
Leveraged return formula: r_leveraged = r + (V_B / V_0) × (r − r_B). Leverage magnifies gains when r > r_B and magnifies losses when r < r_B.

Fair value hierarchy: Level 1 = quoted prices, Level 2 = observable inputs/models, Level 3 = unobservable inputs. Level 3 assets (private equity, real estate) can make returns appear artificially smooth and low-risk.
High-water mark: Performance fees are only charged on returns above the prior peak NAV, protecting investors from paying fees twice on the same gains.
Hard hurdle rate = fees charged only on returns above the hurdle; Soft hurdle rate = once hurdle is exceeded, fees apply to ALL returns. Hard hurdle is more favorable to investors.

Lockup periods prevent redemptions for an initial period; notice periods (30–90 days) give managers time to unwind positions; gates temporarily restrict redemptions during stress; clawbacks require GPs to return excess fees if later performance is poor.

readings.title: Investments In Private Capital: Equity And Debt
readings.summary:
Private capital refers to funding provided to companies outside of public markets, encompassing both private equity and private debt. Private equity funds invest in private companies or take public companies private. The two dominant fund types are leveraged buyout (LBO) funds, which acquire companies using significant debt financing with the goal of improving operations and cash flows, and venture capital (VC) funds, which provide early-stage financing to young companies in exchange for equity or convertible securities. LBO variants include management buyouts (MBOs), where existing management participates, and management buy-ins (MBIs), where new management is brought in.

Venture capital investments are categorized by the portfolio company's development stage. The formative stage includes pre-seed/angel investing, seed-stage financing, and early-stage/start-up financing. Later-stage or expansion VC supports initial growth after production begins. Mezzanine-stage financing prepares a company for an IPO. Private equity firms can also take minority stakes in public companies through private investments in public equity (PIPEs), which allow faster and cheaper capital raises than public offerings.

Private equity firms typically hold portfolio companies for about five years before exiting. Exit strategies include trade sales (selling to a strategic buyer, typically fastest and cheapest), public listings via IPO (highest price but costliest), direct listings (no underwriter, no new capital raised), or SPACs (flexible but dilutive). Other exit routes include recapitalizations (issuing debt to pay dividends—not a true exit), secondary sales to other PE firms, and write-offs for failed investments.

Private debt encompasses loans and fixed-income instruments issued outside public markets. Key categories include direct lending (senior secured loans to mid-market firms), mezzanine debt (subordinated, higher yield, often with equity kickers like warrants), venture debt (loans to VC-backed companies), and distressed debt (buying debt of financially troubled companies at a discount). Distressed debt investors may seek control of the company through a debt-to-equity conversion or simply profit from price recovery.

Private capital investments offer potentially higher returns than public markets but come with significant risks: illiquidity, high leverage, reliance on self-reported valuations, and index biases (survivorship and backfill) that overstate historical returns. Volatility and correlations with other assets may be understated because portfolio companies are revalued infrequently. Investors access private capital primarily through limited partnership structures, where the general partner (GP) manages the fund and limited partners (LPs) provide capital, with fees typically structured as management fees plus carried interest (performance fee).
key_points: LBO funds use high debt to acquire companies and go private; MBO = existing management buys in, MBI = new management replaces existing team.
VC stages in order: Pre-seed (angel) → Seed → Early-stage → Later-stage/Expansion → Mezzanine-stage (pre-IPO); mezzanine STAGE ≠ mezzanine FINANCING (hybrid debt/equity).

Exit strategies ranked by cost/speed: Trade sale (fast, cheap, strategic premium) vs. IPO (highest price, most expensive) vs. Direct listing (no underwriter, no new capital) vs. SPAC (flexible but dilutive). Recapitalization is NOT a true exit.

Private debt categories: Direct lending (senior secured, illiquidity premium), Mezzanine debt (subordinated + equity kicker/warrants), Venture debt (VC-backed firms), Distressed debt (buy at discount, may seek control via debt-to-equity).

Private equity returns are historically higher than public equity but with higher standard deviation; indexes overstate returns due to survivorship bias and backfill bias; volatility/correlation are understated due to infrequent revaluations.
Fund structure: GP manages fund and earns carried interest (typically 20% of profits above hurdle rate) + management fee (~2% of committed capital); LPs provide capital and have limited liability.
PIPE (Private Investment in Public Equity) = private offering to institutional investors by a public company; faster and cheaper than a public offering with fewer disclosure requirements.

readings.title: Real Estate And Infrastructure
readings.summary:
Real estate investment can be mapped along two dimensions: public vs. private and debt vs. equity. Private real estate involves direct ownership or lending, while public real estate involves securities such as REITs, MBSs, CMBSs, and ETFs. Direct (private) investment offers control, diversification benefits, and tax advantages (depreciation deductions), but comes with illiquidity, high capital requirements, complexity, and concentration risk. Public investments offer liquidity and divisibility but sacrifice direct control.

REITs are the dominant vehicle for indirect real estate investment. They avoid double taxation, are exchange-traded (no redemption risk), and are professionally managed. REITs come in three forms: equity REITs (own properties), mortgage REITs (lend or invest in MBSs/CMBSs), and hybrid REITs. Strategy risk ranges from low (core — stable, high-quality properties, open-end structure) to moderate (core-plus, value-add) to high (opportunistic — distressed assets, speculative, closed-end structure).

The risk spectrum for real estate runs from first mortgages and investment-grade CMBSs (most bond-like, least risky) through core strategies (stable rental income, diversified tenant pool) to value-add and opportunistic strategies (most equity-like, highest risk/return). REITs improve a portfolio's risk-return profile, but their correlation with equities is higher than direct real estate — and that correlation rises during market downturns.

Infrastructure investments include transportation (roads, airports, ports, railways), utilities (gas, electric, waste), information/communication technology (telecom towers, cable systems), and social infrastructure (prisons, schools, hospitals). Investors can build and sell/lease assets to governments, acquire existing government assets, or participate via public-private partnerships. Infrastructure cash flows are typically long-duration, inflation-linked, and backed by regulated or concession-based revenue, making them a low-correlation, defensive allocation.
key_points: Real estate quadrant: two dimensions — (1) public vs. private and (2) debt vs. equity. Know examples of each cell (e.g., direct ownership = private equity; REIT shares = public equity; mortgage = private debt; CMBS = public debt).

Direct investment advantages: control, diversification, tax benefits (depreciation deductions). Disadvantages: illiquidity, price opacity, high capital, concentration risk, need for specialized management.

REITs: exempt from double taxation, exchange-traded (no redemption risk), professionally managed. Three types — equity (own property), mortgage (lend/MBS), hybrid. Core REITs = open-end; riskier strategies (core-plus, value-add, opportunistic) = closed-end.
Risk ranking (low to high): first mortgage / investment-grade CMBS → core → core-plus → value-add → opportunistic. First two are bond-like; last two are equity-like.
REIT vs. direct real estate: REITs have higher correlation with equities than direct real estate, and this correlation increases during steep market downturns — a key diversification limitation.

Infrastructure categories: transportation (roads, airports), utilities (gas, electric, waste), ICT (telecom towers, cable), social (prisons, schools, hospitals). Revenue is typically inflation-linked and regulated, making it low-volatility and long-duration.

Infrastructure investment methods: build and sell/lease to government, buy existing government assets (privatization), or public-private partnership (PPP). Cash flows are stable but illiquid, suitable for long-horizon investors like pension funds.

readings.title: Natural Resources Module 82.1: Farmland, Timberland, And
readings.summary:
Natural resources as an investment class encompass raw land, farmland, timberland, and commodities. All three land types are illiquid and location-driven, but farmland and timberland also generate income from their output—crops and timber, respectively. Farmland is primarily held by individuals, while institutions dominate raw land and timberland. Timberland requires specialized expertise and involves larger lot sizes; investors can access it through TIMOs (Timberland Investment Management Organizations). Financing options for farmland and timberland are more limited than for traditional real estate, typically relying on bank loans or private debt, though farmland REITs are emerging for retail investors.

A key distinction between farmland and timberland is harvest flexibility. Agricultural crops must be harvested within a narrow window, so farmers hedge price risk using short positions in commodity futures. Timber, however, can be stored 'on the stump,' giving investors the option to delay harvest when prices are low and accelerate when prices are high. Both farmland and timberland absorb carbon, making them attractive to ESG-focused investors concerned with climate change.

Commodities are divided into metals (base and precious), agricultural products, and energy products. Direct investment is possible but rare; futures and other derivatives are the predominant vehicles. Commodity pricing follows the cost-of-carry model: futures price ≈ spot price × (1 + risk-free rate) + storage costs − convenience yield. When convenience yield is low, futures trade above spot (contango), which hurts long-only investors through negative roll yield. When convenience yield is high, futures trade below spot (backwardation), benefiting long-only investors.

Commodity returns have historically shown higher volatility than global stocks or bonds, but low correlation with both, providing meaningful portfolio diversification. Timberland and farmland offer higher average returns with lower volatility—closer to bonds—making them attractive risk-adjusted alternatives. Critically, commodity prices tend to rise with inflation, making natural resources an effective inflation hedge. Commodity prices are also uniquely sensitive to geopolitical events, weather shocks, and supply inelasticity in the short run.
key_points: Timberland has a 'flexibility option': harvest timing can be delayed when prices are low and accelerated when prices are high — farmland crops cannot be stored this way.

Futures price formula: Futures ≈ Spot × (1 + Rf) + Storage Costs − Convenience Yield. Contango = low convenience yield (futures > spot, hurts long investors). Backwardation = high convenience yield (futures < spot, helps long investors).
Farmland is primarily held by individuals; institutions favor raw land and timberland. TIMOs allow investors without specialized expertise to access timberland.
Commodities have LOW correlation with global equities and bonds → diversification benefit. Commodity prices move WITH inflation → inflation hedge.
Commodity supply is inelastic in the short run (long lead times to change production), making prices highly volatile during demand shocks or supply disruptions (weather, geopolitics).
Investment vehicles for commodities: exchange-traded futures (no counterparty risk), ETPs (ETFs/ETNs), managed futures (CTAs), SMAs for large investors, and specialized sector funds.
Both farmland and timberland absorb carbon → ESG/climate-focused appeal. Newer farmland REITs allow retail investor access to an otherwise illiquid asset class.

readings.title: Hedge Funds Module 83.1: Hedge Funds
readings.summary:
Hedge funds are lightly regulated, private pooled investment vehicles available only to qualified or accredited investors. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs, or REITs (which are publicly traded), hedge funds are privately held and give managers broad freedom to use leverage, derivatives, long/short positions, and a wide universe of securities. Their primary return drivers are market inefficiency and price volatility, and they are typically evaluated on a total or risk-adjusted return basis rather than against a benchmark.

Hedge fund strategies fall into four major categories. Equity hedge strategies (e.g., fundamental long/short, market neutral, short bias) profit from long and short equity positions. Event-driven strategies (e.g., merger arbitrage, distressed/restructuring, activist shareholder) capitalize on corporate events like mergers, bankruptcies, or spin-offs. Relative value strategies (e.g., convertible arbitrage, fixed income arbitrage) exploit pricing discrepancies between related securities. Opportunistic strategies (macro and managed futures) trade based on global economic trends and commodity price dynamics.

Hedge funds have several unique structural characteristics that distinguish them from other investment vehicles. They charge high fees — typically a management fee plus a performance (incentive) fee — often subject to a high-water mark, meaning performance fees are only earned when the fund surpasses its prior peak value. Liquidity is restricted through lockup periods (during which redemptions are prohibited or penalized), notice periods (advance notice required to redeem), and liquidity gates (partial restrictions on redemptions).

Hedge funds can be structured as commingled funds (pooling capital from multiple investors) or as separately managed accounts. Fund-of-funds structures invest in multiple hedge funds, offering diversification and access but adding an additional layer of fees. Compared to private equity, hedge funds tend to invest in more liquid assets and have shorter time horizons with periodic (rather than locked-up) redemptions, though they are still less liquid than publicly traded vehicles.
key_points: Hedge funds are privately held, lightly regulated, and available only to qualified/accredited investors — contrast with publicly traded mutual funds, REITs, and ETFs.
Four main hedge fund strategy categories: (1) Equity hedge, (2) Event-driven, (3) Relative value, (4) Opportunistic (macro/managed futures).
High-water mark: performance fees are only paid when the fund's NAV exceeds its previous peak value — protects investors from paying fees after losses are recovered.
Liquidity restrictions: lockup period (no redemptions allowed initially), notice period (advance notice required), liquidity gate (partial redemption cap) — know each definition.
Merger arbitrage buys the target and shorts the acquirer, but it is NOT risk-free — deals can fall through or terms can change.
Managed futures funds (CTAs) trade commodity futures; commodity prices behave differently from financial assets — high prices reduce demand, low prices reduce supply.

Hedge funds charge higher fees than traditional vehicles (management + incentive fee), have lower transparency to protect proprietary strategies, and often have managers investing their own capital alongside investors.

readings.title: Introduction To Digital Assets
readings.summary:
Digital assets are electronically created, stored, and transferred assets secured by distributed ledger technology (DLT), also called blockchain technology. A distributed ledger is a shared database where all participants hold identical copies of transaction records. Key DLT benefits include accuracy, transparency, rapid ownership transfer, and peer-to-peer interactions, while drawbacks include data privacy concerns and high computational power requirements. Smart contracts are self-executing programs built on DLT that automate contingent claims and collateral transfers based on predetermined conditions.

Blockchains record transactions sequentially in cryptographically linked blocks, each containing a hash of the previous block, making records immutable. The two main consensus protocols are Proof of Work (PoW), where miners solve cryptographic problems to validate transactions (energy-intensive, most widely used), and Proof of Stake (PoS), where validators pledge collateral (stake) to verify transactions and earn returns. Both protocols protect against manipulation; PoW requires controlling 51% of the network to compromise it.

DLT networks are either permissionless (open to all, fully decentralized, like Bitcoin) or permissioned (restricted access with varying levels for participants vs. regulators, more cost-effective). Digital asset categories include cryptocurrencies (privately issued, no central bank backing, supply-limited), stablecoins (pegged to another asset like USD, backed by a reserve basket), meme coins (entertainment-driven, highly speculative), and various token types. Bitcoin and Ether dominate the market, together comprising over 80% of total cryptocurrency market cap as of mid-2022.

Tokens represent a broader category beyond pure currency. Security tokens are digital representations of traditional securities (equity, debt) that can automate dividend and interest payments via smart contracts. Asset-backed tokens maintain price parity with a target asset such as gold. Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) are unique digital assets representing ownership of items like art or collectibles — they are non-interchangeable by design, unlike fungible cryptocurrencies.
key_points: DLT vs. Blockchain: DLT is the broader technology (shared database); blockchain is a specific type of DLT that links records in sequential, cryptographically secured blocks.

Consensus mechanisms: Proof of Work (PoW) = miners solve cryptographic puzzles, energy-intensive, most widely used; Proof of Stake (PoS) = validators stake collateral, earn returns, more energy-efficient.
Permissionless networks (e.g., Bitcoin) are open and decentralized — no central authority, immutable records, trustless. Permissioned networks restrict access and are more cost-effective.

Stablecoins are pegged to another asset (e.g., USD) and backed by a reserve basket to minimize volatility; they cannot be exchanged for fiat currency and have no legal/regulatory backing. Algorithmic stablecoins (e.g., TerraUSD) failed spectacularly in 2022.
Cryptocurrencies have supply limits to act as a store of value, but still experience extreme price volatility. Bitcoin and Ether are the two largest, comprising 80%+ of market cap.

Security tokens are digital representations of traditional securities (equity, debt, real estate) and can automate payments (dividends, interest) via smart contracts — they are subject to securities regulations.
NFTs (Non-Fungible Tokens) are unique, non-interchangeable digital assets proving ownership of a specific item; unlike cryptocurrencies which are fungible (each unit identical and interchangeable).

readings.title: Portfolio Management: An Overview
readings.summary: The portfolio perspective evaluates individual investments by their contribution to overall portfolio risk and return, rather than in isolation. Harry Markowitz's 1950s research showed that diversification reduces portfolio risk without reducing expected return, as long as asset returns are not perfectly positively correlated. This was extended in the 1960s into Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), which links equilibrium expected returns to non-diversifiable (market) risk. The diversification ratio — the standard deviation of an equally-weighted portfolio divided by the average standard deviation of individual securities — quantifies this benefit; a lower ratio means greater risk reduction. Importantly, diversification is least effective during market crises, when correlations tend to rise.
key_points: Diversification ratio = (std dev of equally-weighted portfolio) / (avg std dev of individual securities); a ratio below 1.0 indicates risk-reduction benefit.
The three steps of portfolio management are: Planning (IPS), Execution (asset allocation + security selection), and Feedback (monitoring, rebalancing, performance evaluation).
The Investment Policy Statement (IPS) must specify objectives, constraints, and a benchmark; it should be reviewed at least every few years or when circumstances change materially.
During financial crises, asset correlations increase, reducing diversification benefits — this was evident in the 2008 credit crisis.
Defined benefit (DB) plans promise a specific retirement benefit and bear investment risk with the plan sponsor; defined contribution (DC) plans shift investment risk to the employee.
Endowments and foundations typically have long time horizons, high risk tolerance, and low liquidity needs; banks and insurance companies prioritize low risk and adequate liquidity.
The buy side refers to institutional investors (pension funds, mutual funds, etc.); the sell side refers to broker-dealers and investment banks that facilitate transactions and provide research.

readings.title: Basics Of Portfolio Planning And Construction
readings.summary:
A written Investment Policy Statement (IPS) is the foundation of sound portfolio management. It begins with a joint discussion of the client's risk and return goals, which must be compatible with each other — high returns and low risk are generally mutually exclusive. The IPS documents client circumstances, duties and responsibilities of all parties, investment objectives and constraints, performance benchmarks, permitted asset types, and guidelines for rebalancing. At minimum, every IPS must clearly state client circumstances and constraints, the investment strategy, and a benchmark for evaluating performance.

Risk and return objectives in an IPS can be expressed in absolute or relative terms. Absolute risk objectives set strict limits (e.g., 'no loss exceeding 2% in any 12-month period') or probability-based limits (e.g., 'no greater than 5% probability of returns below -5%'). Relative risk objectives are benchmarked against an index or rate. Return objectives can be stated in nominal or real terms, or relative to a benchmark such as the S&P 500. Peer-based benchmarks (e.g., top-quartile endowment returns) are problematic because they are not investable portfolios and cannot be replicated in advance.

A critical distinction exists between an investor's ability to bear risk and their willingness to bear risk. Ability is objective and based on financial factors: investment horizon, asset/liability ratio, job security, and insurance coverage. Willingness is subjective, rooted in attitudes and beliefs, and often assessed via questionnaire. When the two conflict, the adviser defaults to the lower of the two. If willingness is low but ability is high, the adviser may educate the client, but should not attempt to change the client's fundamental risk personality.

The five key investment constraints are captured by the mnemonic R-R-T-T-L-L-U: Risk, Return, Time horizon, Tax situation, Liquidity, Legal restrictions, and Unique circumstances. Liquidity needs require holding assets that can be converted to cash quickly without significant price concessions — illiquid assets like hedge funds or private equity are unsuitable for investors with unpredictable cash needs. Longer time horizons generally allow for more risk and less liquidity. Tax considerations affect after-tax returns and asset location decisions. Legal and regulatory constraints may restrict certain asset types. Unique circumstances include personal preferences, ESG mandates, or concentrated positions that must be handled individually.
key_points: An IPS must contain at minimum: client circumstances/constraints, investment strategy, and a performance benchmark — memorize the 9 major components (client description, purpose, duties, procedures, objectives, constraints, guidelines, performance evaluation, appendices).

Risk objectives can be absolute (strict limits or probability-based) or relative (benchmarked to an index); return objectives can be nominal, real, or relative — peer benchmarks are NOT valid because they are non-investable.

Ability to bear risk = objective financial factors (longer horizon, more assets, job security, insurance); Willingness = subjective attitudes. When they conflict, always default to the LOWER of the two.
If ability is high but willingness is low, the adviser may educate the investor but must NOT try to change the investor's personality — conform to the lower level.
Use mnemonic R-R-T-T-L-L-U for IPS constraints: Risk, Return, Time horizon, Tax, Liquidity, Legal, Unique circumstances.
Longer time horizon → more risk tolerance and less liquidity required; shorter time horizon → less risk, more liquidity needed.
Illiquid investments (hedge funds, private equity) are unsuitable for investors who may need unexpected access to funds — liquidity constraints must be matched to portfolio asset selection.

readings.title: The Behavioral Biases Of Individuals
readings.summary:
Behavioral finance challenges the traditional finance assumption that individuals are perfectly rational. Pioneered by Kahneman and Tversky in the 1970s, research shows that human decision-making is systematically biased in predictable ways. These biases fall into two broad categories: cognitive errors, which stem from faulty reasoning or information processing and can potentially be corrected through education and awareness; and emotional biases, which arise from feelings and intuition and are much harder to overcome, often requiring accommodation rather than correction. When a bias has both cognitive and emotional elements, focusing on the cognitive component offers the best chance of mitigation.

Cognitive errors are subdivided into belief perseverance biases and information processing errors. Belief perseverance biases include: conservatism (failing to update beliefs appropriately when new information arrives), confirmation (seeking only information that supports existing views), representativeness (categorizing investments based on superficial similarities, including base-rate neglect and sample-size neglect), illusion of control (overestimating one's ability to influence outcomes), and hindsight bias (believing past events were predictable after the fact). These biases cause investors to cling to outdated positions, overweight anecdotal evidence, and distort their assessment of their own track record.

Processing errors represent a second class of cognitive bias in which information is analyzed illogically. Anchoring and adjustment occurs when investors fixate on an initial value and insufficiently update from it. Mental accounting treats money differently depending on its source or intended use, leading to suboptimal portfolio decisions. Framing bias causes decisions to change based on how information is presented rather than its substance. Availability bias leads investors to overweight information that is easily recalled, such as recent dramatic events, when forming probability estimates.

Emotional biases are harder to correct and often must be accommodated in portfolio construction. Loss aversion (feeling losses more acutely than equivalent gains, rooted in prospect theory) leads to holding losers too long and selling winners too soon. Overconfidence bias causes investors to overestimate their forecasting ability and trade excessively. Self-control bias reflects the failure to delay gratification, often leading to insufficient savings. Status quo bias is an inertia preference for the current portfolio state. Endowment bias causes investors to overvalue assets they already own. Regret aversion leads to herding behavior and avoidance of decisions that could result in regret, even when those decisions are rational.

Understanding these biases has direct implications for portfolio management. Advisors should identify a client's dominant biases and decide whether to moderate them (more feasible for cognitive errors) or adapt to them (often necessary for emotional biases). Wealthier investors generally have more latitude to accommodate biases, while investors closer to financial goals require stricter rational discipline. The goal is not to eliminate all bias but to improve decision quality and help clients stick to well-constructed investment plans.
key_points: Cognitive errors (faulty reasoning) CAN be reduced with education; emotional biases (feelings/intuition) are hard to change and often must be ACCOMMODATED — this distinction drives the advisor's response strategy.
Belief perseverance biases (5): Conservatism, Confirmation, Representativeness, Illusion of Control, Hindsight — all involve clinging to or distorting prior beliefs.
Processing errors (4): Anchoring & Adjustment, Mental Accounting, Framing, Availability — all involve systematic flaws in how new information is analyzed or weighted.

Emotional biases (6): Loss Aversion, Overconfidence, Self-Control, Status Quo, Endowment, Regret Aversion — loss aversion (prospect theory) is the most tested; losses hurt ~2x more than equivalent gains feel good.

Representativeness has two subtypes on the exam: base-rate neglect (ignoring population probabilities) and sample-size neglect (over-generalizing from a small sample, e.g., a 3-year fund track record).

Overconfidence has two forms: prediction overconfidence (too-narrow confidence intervals) and certainty overconfidence (overstating probability of being right) — leads to excessive trading and under-diversification.
When a bias is BOTH cognitive and emotional, focus mitigation efforts on the cognitive component — this is explicitly testable as exam guidance.

readings.title: Introduction To Risk Management
readings.summary:
Risk management is not about eliminating risk but about identifying an organization's risk tolerance and aligning actual risk exposures to that tolerance. The process involves identifying, measuring, modifying, and monitoring risks. Because returns above the risk-free rate require taking on risk, the goal is to determine the optimal bundle of risks — increasing exposure where the organization has a comparative advantage and reducing or hedging exposures where it does not.

A comprehensive risk management framework includes establishing risk governance policies, determining risk tolerance, identifying and measuring risks, managing and mitigating risks, monitoring exposures over time, and communicating across the organization. Risk governance is the senior-management-level function that sets the organization's risk tolerance and oversees the entire risk management process, often through a risk management committee that integrates risk considerations across business units.

Risk budgeting translates the overall risk tolerance into a specific allocation of risk across assets or investments. The risk budget can be expressed as a single metric (e.g., portfolio beta, VaR, duration) or broken down by asset class or specific risk factors such as interest rate risk, equity market risk, and foreign exchange risk. The goal is to allocate the acceptable risk budget to the mix of investments expected to generate the highest returns.

Risks are classified as financial or non-financial. Financial risks include credit risk (counterparty default), liquidity risk (forced sale below fair value), and market risk (price and rate uncertainty). Non-financial risks include operational risk, solvency risk, regulatory risk, political/tax risk, legal risk, model risk, tail risk, and accounting risk. These categories often interact — for example, a liquidity crisis can trigger solvency risk, and model risk can cause underestimation of tail risk. For individuals, mortality risk and longevity risk are especially important financial planning considerations.
key_points: Risk management seeks to ALIGN risk exposures to risk tolerance — not minimize or eliminate risk. The firm increases exposure to risks it manages well and reduces exposure to risks it manages poorly.

The seven elements of a risk management framework: (1) risk governance processes, (2) determine risk tolerance, (3) identify and measure risks, (4) manage/mitigate risks, (5) monitor exposures, (6) communicate across the organization, (7) strategic risk analysis.
Risk governance is a senior-management function that sets overall risk tolerance and oversight structure; a risk management committee integrates risk across business units.
Risk budgeting allocates the total acceptable risk to specific assets/investments to maximize expected return; metrics include portfolio beta, VaR, duration, or factor-based measures.
Financial risks: credit risk (counterparty default), liquidity risk (sale below fair value), market risk (prices, rates, currencies).

Non-financial risks to memorize: operational, solvency, regulatory, political/tax, legal, model, tail, and accounting risk. Tail risk specifically refers to extreme outcomes being MORE likely than models predict (often due to incorrectly assuming normality).

Financial and non-financial risks interact — e.g., model risk can cause underestimation of tail risk; a liquidity crunch can cascade into solvency risk. For individuals, mortality risk → life insurance; longevity risk → lifetime annuity.

readings.title: Ethics And Trust In The Investment Profession
readings.summary:
Ethics refers to shared beliefs about acceptable and unacceptable behavior. Ethical conduct balances self-interest with the impact on stakeholders — including clients, coworkers, employers, and the broader profession. A code of ethics is a written set of moral principles that communicates a group's values and sets minimum standards of acceptable behavior, forming the foundation of any true profession.

A profession is defined by specialized knowledge, a code of ethics, a regulatory body, client focus, societal service, and continuing education requirements. These characteristics allow professions to establish trust through high expertise standards, ethical monitoring, and a commitment to putting client interests first. Investment management qualifies as a profession under these criteria, though it faces ongoing challenges in maintaining that trust.

Investment professionals bear special ethical responsibility because they manage clients' wealth — an intangible service whose quality is hard to evaluate. Unethical behavior damages not just individual clients but the entire financial system: it reduces capital flows, increases the cost of capital, causes misallocation of resources, and ultimately harms economic growth and societal well-being. Trust is therefore more critical in finance than in most other industries.

Professionalism in investment management requires adherence to either a suitability standard (matching products to client risk/return needs) or the stronger fiduciary standard (acting in the client's best interest). Ethical challenges arise from overconfidence in one's own ethical judgment, social pressure, loyalty conflicts, short-term incentives, and compliance cultures that ask only 'what can I do?' rather than 'what should I do?'

Legal and ethical standards are related but distinct. Not all unethical acts are illegal, and not all illegal acts are unethical. Ethical principles typically set a higher bar than the law, and new laws often follow perceived ethical failures. The CFA Institute Code and Standards represent an attempt to establish an ethical framework that goes beyond mere legal compliance.
key_points: Ethics = balancing self-interest with impact on stakeholders; ethical conduct improves outcomes for those directly/indirectly affected.
A profession requires: specialized knowledge, code of ethics, regulatory body, client focus, service to society, and continuing education.
Investment professionals are held to either a suitability standard (match to client needs) or the stricter fiduciary standard (act in best interest of client).
Unethical behavior in finance harms the whole economy: reduces investment, raises cost of capital, and causes capital misallocation.
Key challenge: individuals overrate their own ethical behavior; situational factors (social pressure, greed, loyalty) are often stronger drivers of unethical conduct than personal traits.
Rules-based compliance cultures risk producing 'what can I do?' thinking instead of 'what should I do?' — principles-based ethics is preferred.
Legal ≠ ethical: ethical standards set a higher bar than law; new regulations typically follow perceived ethical failures (e.g., Securities Acts of 1933/1934).

readings.title: Code Of Ethics And Standards Of Professional Conduct
readings.summary:
The CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program operates under the CFA Institute Bylaws and Rules of Procedure, governed by the Board of Governors and its Disciplinary Review Committee. Inquiries into member or candidate conduct can be triggered by self-disclosure on annual Professional Conduct Statements, written complaints, evidence from public sources, exam proctor reports, or CFA Institute's own monitoring of exam materials and social media. Once an inquiry begins, staff may interview parties, collect documents, and ultimately issue no sanction, a cautionary letter, or a formal disciplinary sanction — which the member may accept or appeal to a peer review panel.

The Code of Ethics sets six foundational obligations for all CFA members and candidates: act with integrity and in an ethical manner; place the integrity of the profession and client interests above personal interests; use reasonable care and independent judgment; practice and encourage ethical behavior; promote integrity of global capital markets; and maintain and improve professional competence. These principles underpin everything that follows in the Standards.

The seven Standards of Professional Conduct provide specific rules across key domains. Standard I (Professionalism) covers knowledge of law, independence and objectivity, misrepresentation, and misconduct. Standard II (Integrity of Capital Markets) prohibits acting on material nonpublic information and market manipulation. Standard III (Duties to Clients) addresses loyalty, prudence, fair dealing, suitability, performance presentation, and confidentiality. Standard IV covers duties to employers including loyalty and whistleblowing, while Standard V governs investment analysis and communications. Standard VI addresses conflicts of interest including disclosure and referral fees, and Standard VII covers responsibilities as a CFA member or candidate including conduct during exams.

A critical hierarchy applies throughout: when laws conflict with the Code and Standards, members must follow whichever is stricter. Members must also dissociate from any violation they cannot prevent. Confidentiality of client information must be preserved except when illegal activity is involved, required by law, or the client permits disclosure — making the exceptions just as testable as the rule itself.
key_points: Memorize the 6 Code of Ethics components: integrity, client-first, independent judgment, ethical practice, promote market integrity, maintain competence.

Memorize the 7 Standards in order: I-Professionalism, II-Capital Market Integrity, III-Duties to Clients, IV-Duties to Employers, V-Investment Analysis, VI-Conflicts of Interest, VII-CFA Member/Candidate Responsibilities.
Standard I(A) hierarchy rule: comply with the MORE STRICT of applicable law or the Code and Standards; never assist or participate in a violation — dissociate.
Standard II(A) on material nonpublic information: members must not act OR cause others to act on such information (both direct trading and tipping are prohibited).
Standard III(C) Suitability applies differently in advisory relationships (assess client profile before acting) vs. portfolio mandates (stay within stated strategy constraints).
Confidentiality exceptions under Standard III(E): disclose if client activity is illegal, if disclosure is required by law, or if the client gives permission.
Inquiry triggers to know: self-disclosure on annual statements, written complaints, public sources/media, exam proctor reports, and CFA Institute's own exam/social media monitoring.

readings.title: Guidance For Standards I–VII Module 91.1: Guidance For Standards I(A)
readings.summary:
The Standards of Professional Conduct consist of seven Standards (I–VII) with 22 subsections, and together with the Code of Ethics form the ethical backbone of CFA Institute membership. The Standards use terms like 'reasonable,' 'adequate,' and 'prudent,' but exam questions are written so that the correct answer does not hinge on borderline interpretations — violations will be made clear. Members must focus on recognizing outright violations rather than debating grey-area definitions.

Standard I(A) — Knowledge of the Law — requires members and candidates to understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations governing their professional activities, including the CFA Institute Code and Standards. When multiple regulatory regimes apply (home country, host country, and the Standards), members must follow the most strict set of rules. Any violation of the Code and Standards automatically also violates Standard I(A).

When a member becomes aware of ongoing violations by coworkers or clients, the preferred path is to report the situation to a supervisor or compliance department. If the firm fails to act, the member must dissociate from the violating activity — for example, stopping use of misleading marketing materials or refusing to work with a group that misallocates trades. In extreme cases where dissociation is impossible, resignation may be required. Notably, the Standards do not require members to report wrongdoers externally, though members are strongly encouraged to report violations to CFA Institute's Professional Conduct Program, and local law may independently impose a reporting obligation.

Best practices for members include keeping current on relevant laws and regulations, maintaining copies of applicable rules, consulting compliance personnel or legal counsel when in doubt, and documenting any violations observed during a dissociation. Firms are recommended to maintain a written code of ethics, provide employees with ongoing regulatory education, and establish clear procedures for reporting suspected violations internally.
key_points: When multiple sets of rules apply (CFA Standards, country of residence, country of business), always follow the MOST STRICT — never violate any one of the three.
Any violation of the Code and Standards is automatically also a violation of Standard I(A) — Knowledge of the Law.

Members are NOT required by the Standards to report violations externally, but are 'strongly encouraged' to report to CFA Institute's Professional Conduct Program; local law may separately require reporting.
If a member cannot stop a violation from within (supervisor or compliance won't act), they must DISSOCIATE — and in extreme cases must resign from the firm.

Intent generally does not excuse a violation: if an action violates the Standard, the reason or motivation behind it does not matter (exception: Standards where intent is an explicit element, e.g., intent to mislead).

Exam tip on gifts: 'token' has no defined dollar amount in the Standards, but clue words like 'lavish,' 'luxury,' or specific examples (Super Bowl tickets, week at a condo) signal a non-token gift that likely violates the Standards.
Recommended member practice: when dissociating from violations, keep detailed records documenting the violations and encourage the employer to end the violating conduct.

readings.title: Introduction To The Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS)
readings.summary:
GIPS (Global Investment Performance Standards) were created to address the problem of inconsistent and potentially misleading performance reporting by investment management firms. Without a common standard, firms could cherry-pick top-performing portfolios, exclude terminated accounts (often closed due to poor performance), or select favorable time periods — all of which inflate reported results and make cross-firm comparisons meaningless. GIPS provide a standardized methodology that ensures fair, comparable, and transparent performance reporting for the benefit of current clients, prospective clients, and regulatory oversight bodies.

Compliance with GIPS is voluntary, but firms that claim compliance must do so fully and on a firmwide basis — partial compliance is not permitted. Only investment management firms (those that manage assets for others) may claim GIPS compliance. Related parties such as software vendors or consultants may endorse GIPS but cannot claim compliance. The GIPS standards are organized into eight sections covering fundamentals of compliance, input data and calculation methodology, composite and pooled fund maintenance, four reporting sections (composite and pooled fund reports using time-weighted or money-weighted returns), and advertising guidelines.

A central concept in GIPS is the composite — a grouping of all fee-paying, discretionary portfolios managed according to the same investment strategy or objective. Composite returns must be asset-weighted averages of the included portfolios, not simple averages. Critically, firms must assign each portfolio to a composite before its performance is known, preventing retroactive selection bias. All fee-paying discretionary portfolios must belong to at least one composite.

The definition of the firm for GIPS purposes must reflect how the business is held out to clients — including all geographic locations operating under the same name. Discretion determines whether a portfolio qualifies for composite inclusion; if client-imposed restrictions prevent the manager from implementing the intended strategy, the portfolio may be classified as nondiscretionary and excluded. Independent third-party verification, while not required, is strongly encouraged. Verification covers the entire firm's practices — not individual composites — and the verifier must confirm both that composite construction follows GIPS and that the firm's processes support compliant performance presentation.
key_points: GIPS compliance is VOLUNTARY, but firms claiming compliance must comply FULLY and on a FIRMWIDE basis — no partial compliance allowed.
Only investment management firms (asset managers) can CLAIM GIPS compliance; related parties (e.g., software vendors) may only ENDORSE it.
Composites must include ALL fee-paying, discretionary portfolios managed under the same strategy — past and present (including terminated accounts).
Composite returns are ASSET-WEIGHTED averages of constituent portfolio returns, NOT simple averages.
Portfolios must be assigned to composites BEFORE performance is known to prevent cherry-picking; a portfolio deemed nondiscretionary (due to client restrictions) may be excluded.
Independent verification is NOT required for GIPS compliance, but if pursued, it must be done by a THIRD PARTY on a FIRMWIDE basis — not on individual composites.
The three classic pre-GIPS abuses to recognize: (1) selecting only top-performing portfolios, (2) excluding terminated accounts, (3) choosing favorable reporting time periods.

readings.title: Ethics Application Module 93.1: Ethics Application
readings.summary:
Reading 93 applies the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct to real-world scenarios across all seven Standards. Rather than presenting abstract rules, it works through concrete cases that illustrate both clear violations and nuanced situations where conduct may or may not cross the line. The cases span a wide range of professional behaviors—from forging signatures and misrepresenting returns to handling insider information and protecting client confidentiality.

Standard I (Professionalism) cases highlight that members must actively disassociate from ongoing violations they cannot stop, must investigate suspicious activity regardless of a client's relationships or tenure, and must never misrepresent facts—including by omission. Guaranteeing returns, failing to disclose key personnel departures, and posting false information on social media are all clear violations. Civil disobedience arrests related to personal beliefs, however, are not automatically misconduct violations.

Standards II and III cases emphasize the breadth of market integrity and client duty obligations. Material nonpublic information includes overheard conversations and selectively shared regulatory data. Duties to clients cannot be contractually waived—members cannot 'opt out' of the Standards. Fair dealing requires simultaneous dissemination of recommendation changes, though tiered services (e.g., premium update subscriptions) are permissible if disclosed to all clients. Suitability applies even when clients self-direct their requests, and performance records must accurately represent the fund being presented.

Standards IV through VII address employer duties, investment conduct, and conflicts of interest. Members must not disparage current employers while still employed, cannot take client records when leaving, and must maintain independence when making investment recommendations—including disclosing any personal holdings. Referral fees must be disclosed; research must be grounded in reasonable basis; and proxy voting must serve client interests, not be delegated away without oversight. The overarching theme is that no relationship, convenience, or financial pressure justifies departing from the Standards.
key_points: Disassociation is required when a member cannot stop an ongoing violation—continuing to work with affected clients constitutes participation in the violation (Standard I(A)).
Misrepresentation includes omission: failing to disclose a key personnel departure while a proposal is under consideration violates Standard I(C), even if all other information is accurate.

Members cannot contractually waive their duties under the Standards—client agreements that exclude best-interest obligations or restrict clients' legal rights are themselves violations of Standard III(A).
Selective disclosure to a subset of analysts renders information 'nonpublic'—material information shared only in an analyst meeting cannot be acted upon or redistributed under Standard II(A).
Performance presentation must reflect the actual fund being marketed; using pre-fund composite returns to imply a longer track record is a violation of Standard III(D).

Tiered service offerings (e.g., premium update subscriptions) are permissible under Fair Dealing only if all clients are informed of the availability of the service and no actual recommendation changes are embedded in the premium tier.

Downloading client PII to a personal server, even for convenience, violates the Preservation of Confidentiality standard—and the compliance officer who allowed it may also be liable (Standard III(E)).
